自然科学がそうして、意味を排除してきてしまったため、そこにおける自然観というのは、内容がすっかり貧弱になった。例えば、自然科学の自然観というのには倫理的な要素がすっかり欠落してしまっている<ref name='josetsu' />。
自然科学を信仰する人は、自然哲学を時代遅れだなどと見なすことがある<ref name='josetsu' />。だが事態はそう簡単ではない。自然科学的な思考でものごとをごり押ししてしまった結果、[[環境問題]]が起きてしまった<ref name='josetsu' />。これについて自然科学では根本的反省も行われないし、是非善悪についての詳細の規定もたてられない<ref name='josetsu' />。
[[環境問題]]のように、人間の生きざまの問題は、[[道徳]]や[[倫理]]、つまり哲学的なことに関係がある以上、こうしたことがらを扱う時には、自然科学的な思考を当てにするのではなく、哲学的に考える必要があるのである <ref name='josetsu' />。
=== 個人的なもの === [[生物学]]、特に[[生態学]]など、自然に深くかかわり野外研究的な活動を長年続けてきた人が、自然に関するその信条を述べるのに「自然観」の語を使う例もある。たとえば[[今西錦司]]は『私の自然観』（講談社文庫、1978年）を出版している。
==現代における自然哲学==
== 出典・脚注 == <references/>
{{DEFAULTSORT:しせんかん}} [[Category:観点]] [[Category:自然]]

'''Shizenkan''' (自然観) refers to the perception of {{ill|Nature|ja|自然}} as a fundamental value system. It is believed that there are significant differences depending on {{ill|Culture|ja|文化|1=Bunka|2=ja|3=文化 (元号)|4=de|5=Bunka (Ära)|6=ko|7=분카}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Droz|first=Layna|last2=Chen|first2=Hsun-Mei|last3=Chu|first3=Hung-Tao|last4=Fajrini|first4=Rika|last5=Imbong|first5=Jerry|last6=Jannel|first6=Romaric|last7=Komatsubara|first7=Orika|last8=Lagasca-Hiloma|first8=Concordia Marie A.|last9=Meas|first9=Chansatya|date=2022-05-31|title=Exploring the diversity of conceptualizations of nature in East and South-East Asia|url=https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-022-01186-5|journal=Humanities and Social Sciences Communications|volume=9|issue=1|pages=1–12|language=en|doi=10.1057/s41599-022-01186-5|issn=2662-9992}}</ref>
== Mythological Views of Nature == Among the earliest conceptions of nature are those described in the mythologies of various societies, which illustrate the relationship between humans and nature.
A unique aspect of mythological perceptions of nature is that they also explain history. This is a strength that surpasses even {{ill|Philosophical Views of Nature|ja|自然哲学}}, which tend to avoid historical explanations.<ref name='josetsu'>{{Cite book|title=Shizen Tetsugaku Josetsu|author=Imamichi Tomonobu|publisher=Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|year=1993}}</ref>
In the {{ill|Judeo-Christian tradition|ja|ユダヤ教・キリスト教圏}}, references to nature can be found in the {{ill|Old Testament|ja|旧約聖書}} and {{ill|New Testament|ja|新約聖書}}, while in the Islamic world, such references appear in the {{ill|Quran|ja|コーラン}}. In Japan, natural perceptions from the {{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集}}, compiled in ancient times, provide insight into the perspectives of that era.
In {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシャ神話}}, nature is depicted through various deities. The wind, for example, had different gods: the north wind was ruled by {{ill|Boreas|ja|ボレアス}}, and the west wind by {{ill|Zephyrus|ja|ゼフュリス}}. Similarly, fire was governed by its own deity, and even hearth fires had separate divine representations.
== Natural Philosophy == Natural philosophy seeks to discover logical structures and laws governing nature.<ref name='josetsu' /> This field of philosophy extends broadly and, in some contexts, encompasses the entirety of philosophical thought.
{{main|Natural philosophy}}
=== Eastern Views === In {{ill|Chinese philosophy|ja|中国哲学}}, it was believed that "pure and light substances rise to form the heavens, while impure and heavy substances descend to form the earth." When applied to the classical {{ill|Four Elements|ja|四大}}, wind and fire were considered light and rising, while the earth element was regarded as heavy.<ref name='josetsu' />
The {{ill|Liezi|ja|列子}}, attributed to {{ill|Liezi|ja|列子}}, posited that everything initially existed in a chaotic state of {{ill|Qi|ja|気}}.<ref name='josetsu' />
China also developed the concept of {{ill|Five Elements|ja|五行}}, which consists of wood, fire, earth, metal, and water.<ref name='josetsu' /> This theory, rather than defining elements in a material sense, is often interpreted as representing dynamic phenomena.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Western Views === According to {{ill|Aristotle|ja|アリストテレス}}, the first philosopher to engage in naturalistic inquiry was {{ill|Thales|ja|タレース}}, who is considered the originator of "Why?" philosophy. Thales proposed that the fundamental principle, or {{ill|Arche|ja|アルケー}}, was water.<ref name='josetsu' />
{{ill|Anaximander|ja|アナクシマンドロス}} proposed that the Arche was "the infinite (Apeiron)," while {{ill|Anaximenes|ja|アナクシメネース}} believed it to be "air." {{ill|Heraclitus|ja|ヘラクレイトス}} asserted that fire was the fundamental principle.<ref name='josetsu' /> These perspectives are sometimes regarded as forms of {{ill|Monism|ja|一元論}}.<ref name='josetsu' />
The French philosopher {{ill|Blaise Pascal|ja|ブレーズ・パスカル}} positioned humans between angels and beasts, viewing them as intermediaries between the supernatural and the natural world.<ref>''Pensées'' 358</ref><ref name='josetsu' />
== Natural Science == By the 18th century, the rise of {{ill|Natural science|ja|自然科学}} led to a mode of inquiry that excluded subjective meaning and consciousness. Many scholars have noted this as a key characteristic of modern science.
Due to this exclusion, scientific conceptions of nature often appear impoverished. For example, ethical dimensions are absent from scientific understandings of nature.<ref name='josetsu' />
Some proponents of natural science dismiss natural philosophy as outdated.<ref name='josetsu' /> However, the unchecked application of scientific thinking has contributed to global issues such as {{ill|Environmental problems|ja|環境問題}}.<ref name='josetsu' /> Natural science lacks the means for deep ethical reflection and moral guidance.<ref name='josetsu' />
Since issues like environmental destruction are fundamentally ethical, they require philosophical rather than purely scientific approaches.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Personal Views === Some scientists and researchers in {{ill|Biology|ja|生物学}}, particularly {{ill|Ecology|ja|生態学}}, use the term "shizenkan" to describe their personal perspectives on nature. For example, {{ill|Kinji Imanishi|ja|今西錦司}} published ''Watashi no Shizenkan'' (My View of Nature) in 1978.
== Modern Natural Philosophy ==

[[ja:%E8%87%AA%E7%84%B6%E8%A6%B3]] [[en:Natural cycles]] [[sn:Natural cycles]] [[sn:atural cycles]] [[ja:自然観]]

'''Shizenkan''' (自然観) refers to the perception of {{ill|Nature|ja|自然|qq=draft}} as a fundamental value system. It is believed that there are significant differences depending on {{ill|Culture|ja|文化|1=Bunka|2=ja|3=文化 (元号)|4=de|5=Bunka (Ära)|6=ko|7=분카|qq=draft}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Droz|first=Layna|last2=Chen|first2=Hsun-Mei|last3=Chu|first3=Hung-Tao|last4=Fajrini|first4=Rika|last5=Imbong|first5=Jerry|last6=Jannel|first6=Romaric|last7=Komatsubara|first7=Orika|last8=Lagasca-Hiloma|first8=Concordia Marie A.|last9=Meas|first9=Chansatya|date=2022-05-31|title=Exploring the diversity of conceptualizations of nature in East and South-East Asia|url=https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-022-01186-5|journal=Humanities and Social Sciences Communications|volume=9|issue=1|pages=1–12|language=en|doi=10.1057/s41599-022-01186-5|issn=2662-9992}}</ref>
== Mythological Views of Nature == Among the earliest conceptions of nature are those described in the mythologies of various societies, which illustrate the relationship between humans and nature.
A unique aspect of mythological perceptions of nature is that they also explain history. This is a strength that surpasses even {{ill|Philosophical Views of Nature|ja|自然哲学|qq=draft}}, which tend to avoid historical explanations.<ref name='josetsu'>{{Cite book|title=Shizen Tetsugaku Josetsu|author=Imamichi Tomonobu|publisher=Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|year=1993}}</ref>
In the {{ill|Judeo-Christian tradition|ja|ユダヤ教・キリスト教圏|qq=draft}}, references to nature can be found in the {{ill|Old Testament|ja|旧約聖書|qq=draft}} and {{ill|New Testament|ja|新約聖書|qq=draft}}, while in the Islamic world, such references appear in the {{ill|Quran|ja|コーラン|qq=draft}}. In Japan, natural perceptions from the {{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集|qq=draft}}, compiled in ancient times, provide insight into the perspectives of that era.
In {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシャ神話|qq=draft}}, nature is depicted through various deities. The wind, for example, had different gods: the north wind was ruled by {{ill|Boreas|ja|ボレアス|qq=draft}}, and the west wind by {{ill|Zephyrus|ja|ゼフュリス|qq=draft}}. Similarly, fire was governed by its own deity, and even hearth fires had separate divine representations.
== Natural Philosophy == Natural philosophy seeks to discover logical structures and laws governing nature.<ref name='josetsu' /> This field of philosophy extends broadly and, in some contexts, encompasses the entirety of philosophical thought.
{{main|Natural philosophy}}
=== Eastern Views === In {{ill|Chinese philosophy|ja|中国哲学|qq=draft}}, it was believed that "pure and light substances rise to form the heavens, while impure and heavy substances descend to form the earth." When applied to the classical {{ill|Four Elements|ja|四大|qq=draft}}, wind and fire were considered light and rising, while the earth element was regarded as heavy.<ref name='josetsu' />
The {{ill|Liezi|ja|列子|qq=draft}}, attributed to {{ill|Liezi|ja|列子|qq=draft}}, posited that everything initially existed in a chaotic state of {{ill|Qi|ja|気|qq=draft}}.<ref name='josetsu' />
China also developed the concept of {{ill|Five Elements|ja|五行|qq=draft}}, which consists of wood, fire, earth, metal, and water.<ref name='josetsu' /> This theory, rather than defining elements in a material sense, is often interpreted as representing dynamic phenomena.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Western Views === According to {{ill|Aristotle|ja|アリストテレス|qq=draft}}, the first philosopher to engage in naturalistic inquiry was {{ill|Thales|ja|タレース|qq=draft}}, who is considered the originator of "Why?" philosophy. Thales proposed that the fundamental principle, or {{ill|Arche|ja|アルケー|qq=draft}}, was water.<ref name='josetsu' />
{{ill|Anaximander|ja|アナクシマンドロス|qq=draft}} proposed that the Arche was "the infinite (Apeiron)," while {{ill|Anaximenes|ja|アナクシメネース|qq=draft}} believed it to be "air." {{ill|Heraclitus|ja|ヘラクレイトス|qq=draft}} asserted that fire was the fundamental principle.<ref name='josetsu' /> These perspectives are sometimes regarded as forms of {{ill|Monism|ja|一元論|qq=draft}}.<ref name='josetsu' />
The French philosopher {{ill|Blaise Pascal|ja|ブレーズ・パスカル|qq=draft}} positioned humans between angels and beasts, viewing them as intermediaries between the supernatural and the natural world.<ref>''Pensées'' 358</ref><ref name='josetsu' />
== Natural Science == By the 18th century, the rise of {{ill|Natural science|ja|自然科学|qq=draft}} led to a mode of inquiry that excluded subjective meaning and consciousness. Many scholars have noted this as a key characteristic of modern science.
Due to this exclusion, scientific conceptions of nature often appear impoverished. For example, ethical dimensions are absent from scientific understandings of nature.<ref name='josetsu' />
Some proponents of natural science dismiss natural philosophy as outdated.<ref name='josetsu' /> However, the unchecked application of scientific thinking has contributed to global issues such as {{ill|Environmental problems|ja|環境問題|qq=draft}}.<ref name='josetsu' /> Natural science lacks the means for deep ethical reflection and moral guidance.<ref name='josetsu' />
Since issues like environmental destruction are fundamentally ethical, they require philosophical rather than purely scientific approaches.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Personal Views === Some scientists and researchers in {{ill|Biology|ja|生物学|qq=draft}}, particularly {{ill|Ecology|ja|生態学|qq=draft}}, use the term "shizenkan" to describe their personal perspectives on nature. For example, {{ill|Kinji Imanishi|ja|今西錦司|qq=draft}} published ''Watashi no Shizenkan'' (My View of Nature) in 1978.
== Modern Natural Philosophy ==

Thales proposed that the fundamental principle, or {{ill|Arche|ja|アルケー|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arche|qq=}}, was water.<ref name='josetsu' />
{{ill|Anaximander|ja|アナクシマンドロス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anaximander|qq=}} proposed that the Arche was "the infinite (Apeiron)," while {{ill|Anaximenes|ja|アナクシメネース|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anaximenes|qq=}} believed it to be "air." {{ill|Heraclitus|ja|ヘラクレイトス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heraclitus|qq=}} asserted that fire was the fundamental principle.<ref name='josetsu' /> These perspectives are sometimes regarded as forms of {{ill|Monism|ja|一元論|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monism|qq=}}.<ref name='josetsu' />
The French philosopher {{ill|Blaise Pascal|ja|ブレーズ・パスカル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Blaise Pascal|qq=}} positioned humans between angels and beasts, viewing them as intermediaries between the supernatural and the natural world.<ref>''Pensées'' 358</ref><ref name='josetsu' />
== Natural Science == By the 18th century, the rise of {{ill|Natural science|ja|自然科学|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Natural science|qq=}} led to a mode of inquiry that excluded subjective meaning and consciousness. Many scholars have noted this as a key characteristic of modern science.
Due to this exclusion, scientific conceptions of nature often appear impoverished. For example, ethical dimensions are absent from scientific understandings of nature.<ref name='josetsu' />
Some proponents of natural science dismiss natural philosophy as outdated.<ref name='josetsu' /> However, the unchecked application of scientific thinking has contributed to global issues such as {{ill|Environmental problems|ja|環境問題|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Environmental problems|qq=}}.<ref name='josetsu' /> Natural science lacks the means for deep ethical reflection and moral guidance.<ref name='josetsu' />
Since issues like environmental destruction are fundamentally ethical, they require philosophical rather than purely scientific approaches.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Personal Views === Some scientists and researchers in {{ill|Biology|ja|生物学|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Biology|qq=}}, particularly {{ill|Ecology|ja|生態学|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ecology|qq=}}, use the term "shizenkan" to describe their personal perspectives on nature. For example, {{ill|Kinji Imanishi|ja|今西錦司|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinji Imanishi|qq=}} published ''Watashi no Shizenkan'' (My View of Nature) in 1978.
== Modern Natural Philosophy ==

revision.comment: Bot: remove sole member from [[Category:Natural cycles]]
revision.text:
'''Shizenkan''' (自然観) refers to the perception of {{ill|Nature|ja|自然|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nature|qq=}} as a fundamental value system. It is believed that there are significant differences depending on {{ill|Culture|ja|文化|1=Bunka|2=ja|3=文化 (元号)|4=de|5=Bunka (Ära)|6=ko|7=분카|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Culture|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunka|qq=}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Droz|first=Layna|last2=Chen|first2=Hsun-Mei|last3=Chu|first3=Hung-Tao|last4=Fajrini|first4=Rika|last5=Imbong|first5=Jerry|last6=Jannel|first6=Romaric|last7=Komatsubara|first7=Orika|last8=Lagasca-Hiloma|first8=Concordia Marie A.|last9=Meas|first9=Chansatya|date=2022-05-31|title=Exploring the diversity of conceptualizations of nature in East and South-East Asia|url=https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-022-01186-5|journal=Humanities and Social Sciences Communications|volume=9|issue=1|pages=1–12|language=en|doi=10.1057/s41599-022-01186-5|issn=2662-9992}}</ref>
== Mythological Views of Nature == Among the earliest conceptions of nature are those described in the mythologies of various societies, which illustrate the relationship between humans and nature.
A unique aspect of mythological perceptions of nature is that they also explain history. This is a strength that surpasses even {{ill|Philosophical Views of Nature|ja|自然哲学|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Philosophical Views of Nature|qq=}}, which tend to avoid historical explanations.<ref name='josetsu'>{{Cite book|title=Shizen Tetsugaku Josetsu|author=Imamichi Tomonobu|publisher=Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|year=1993}}</ref>
In the {{ill|Judeo-Christian tradition|ja|ユダヤ教・キリスト教圏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Judeo-Christian tradition|qq=}}, references to nature can be found in the {{ill|Old Testament|ja|旧約聖書|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Testament|qq=}} and {{ill|New Testament|ja|新約聖書|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Testament|qq=}}, while in the Islamic world, such references appear in the {{ill|Quran|ja|コーラン|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Quran|qq=}}. In Japan, natural perceptions from the {{ill|Manyoshu|ja|万葉集|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manyoshu|qq=}}, compiled in ancient times, provide insight into the perspectives of that era.
In {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシャ神話|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greek mythology|qq=}}, nature is depicted through various deities. The wind, for example, had different gods: the north wind was ruled by {{ill|Boreas|ja|ボレアス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Boreas|qq=}}, and the west wind by {{ill|Zephyrus|ja|ゼフュリス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zephyrus|qq=}}. Similarly, fire was governed by its own deity, and even hearth fires had separate divine representations.
== Natural Philosophy == Natural philosophy seeks to discover logical structures and laws governing nature.<ref name='josetsu' /> This field of philosophy extends broadly and, in some contexts, encompasses the entirety of philosophical thought.
{{main|Natural philosophy}}
=== Eastern Views === In {{ill|Chinese philosophy|ja|中国哲学|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinese philosophy|qq=}}, it was believed that "pure and light substances rise to form the heavens, while impure and heavy substances descend to form the earth." When applied to the classical {{ill|Four Elements|ja|四大|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Elements|qq=}}, wind and fire were considered light and rising, while the earth element was regarded as heavy.<ref name='josetsu' />
The {{ill|Liezi|ja|列子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Liezi|qq=}}, attributed to {{ill|Liezi|ja|列子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Liezi|qq=}}, posited that everything initially existed in a chaotic state of {{ill|Qi|ja|気|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qi|qq=}}.<ref name='josetsu' />
China also developed the concept of {{ill|Five Elements|ja|五行思想|1=Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|2=ja|3=五行思想|lt=Five Elements (Wuxing)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Five Elements|qq=}}, which consists of wood, fire, earth, metal, and water.<ref name='josetsu' /> This theory, rather than defining elements in a material sense, is often interpreted as representing dynamic phenomena.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Western Views === According to {{ill|Aristotle|ja|アリストテレス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aristotle|qq=}}, the first philosopher to engage in naturalistic inquiry was {{ill|Thales|ja|タレース|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thales|qq=}}, who is considered the originator of "Why?" philosophy. Thales proposed that the fundamental principle, or {{ill|Arche|ja|アルケー|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arche|qq=}}, was water.<ref name='josetsu' />
{{ill|Anaximander|ja|アナクシマンドロス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anaximander|qq=}} proposed that the Arche was "the infinite (Apeiron)," while {{ill|Anaximenes|ja|アナクシメネース|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anaximenes|qq=}} believed it to be "air." {{ill|Heraclitus|ja|ヘラクレイトス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heraclitus|qq=}} asserted that fire was the fundamental principle.<ref name='josetsu' /> These perspectives are sometimes regarded as forms of {{ill|Monism|ja|一元論|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Monism|qq=}}.<ref name='josetsu' />
The French philosopher {{ill|Blaise Pascal|ja|ブレーズ・パスカル|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Blaise Pascal|qq=}} positioned humans between angels and beasts, viewing them as intermediaries between the supernatural and the natural world.<ref>''Pensées'' 358</ref><ref name='josetsu' />
== Natural Science == By the 18th century, the rise of {{ill|Natural science|ja|自然科学|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Natural science|qq=}} led to a mode of inquiry that excluded subjective meaning and consciousness. Many scholars have noted this as a key characteristic of modern science.
Due to this exclusion, scientific conceptions of nature often appear impoverished. For example, ethical dimensions are absent from scientific understandings of nature.<ref name='josetsu' />
Some proponents of natural science dismiss natural philosophy as outdated.<ref name='josetsu' /> However, the unchecked application of scientific thinking has contributed to global issues such as {{ill|Environmental problems|ja|環境問題|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Environmental problems|qq=}}.<ref name='josetsu' /> Natural science lacks the means for deep ethical reflection and moral guidance.<ref name='josetsu' />
Since issues like environmental destruction are fundamentally ethical, they require philosophical rather than purely scientific approaches.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Personal Views === Some scientists and researchers in {{ill|Biology|ja|生物学|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Biology|qq=}}, particularly {{ill|Ecology|ja|生態学|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ecology|qq=}}, use the term "shizenkan" to describe their personal perspectives on nature. For example, {{ill|Kinji Imanishi|ja|今西錦司|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinji Imanishi|qq=}} published ''Watashi no Shizenkan'' (My View of Nature) in 1978.
== Modern Natural Philosophy ==

== Mythological Views of Nature == Among the earliest conceptions of nature are those described in the mythologies of various societies, which illustrate the relationship between humans and nature.
A unique aspect of mythological perceptions of nature is that they also explain history. This is a strength that surpasses even [[:en:Natural philosophy|Philosophical Views of Nature]], which tend to avoid historical explanations.<ref name='josetsu'>{{Cite book|title=Shizen Tetsugaku Josetsu|author=Imamichi Tomonobu|publisher=Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|year=1993}}</ref>
In the {{ill|Judeo-Christian tradition|ja|ユダヤ教・キリスト教圏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Judeo-Christian tradition|qq=}}, references to nature can be found in the [[:en:Old Testament|Old Testament]] and [[:en:New Testament|New Testament]], while in the Islamic world, such references appear in the {{ill|Quran|ja|コーラン|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Quran|qq=}}. In Japan, natural perceptions from the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Manyoshu]], compiled in ancient times, provide insight into the perspectives of that era.
In {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシャ神話|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greek mythology|qq=}}, nature is depicted through various deities. The wind, for example, had different gods: the north wind was ruled by [[:en:Boreas (disambiguation)|Boreas]], and the west wind by {{ill|Zephyrus|ja|ゼフュリス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zephyrus|qq=}}. Similarly, fire was governed by its own deity, and even hearth fires had separate divine representations.
== Natural Philosophy == Natural philosophy seeks to discover logical structures and laws governing nature.<ref name='josetsu' /> This field of philosophy extends broadly and, in some contexts, encompasses the entirety of philosophical thought.
{{main|Natural philosophy}}
=== Eastern Views === In [[:en:Chinese philosophy|Chinese philosophy]], it was believed that "pure and light substances rise to form the heavens, while impure and heavy substances descend to form the earth." When applied to the classical [[:en:Big Four|Four Elements]], wind and fire were considered light and rising, while the earth element was regarded as heavy.<ref name='josetsu' />
The [[:en:Liezi|Liezi]], attributed to [[:en:Liezi|Liezi]], posited that everything initially existed in a chaotic state of [[:en:Qi|Qi]].<ref name='josetsu' />
China also developed the concept of [[:en:Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|Five Elements (Wuxing)]], which consists of wood, fire, earth, metal, and water.<ref name='josetsu' /> This theory, rather than defining elements in a material sense, is often interpreted as representing dynamic phenomena.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Western Views === According to [[:en:Aristotle|Aristotle]], the first philosopher to engage in naturalistic inquiry was {{ill|Thales|ja|タレース|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thales|qq=}}, who is considered the originator of "Why?" philosophy. Thales proposed that the fundamental principle, or {{ill|Arche|ja|アルケー|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arche|qq=}}, was water.<ref name='josetsu' />
[[:en:Anaximander|Anaximander]] proposed that the Arche was "the infinite (Apeiron)," while {{ill|Anaximenes|ja|アナクシメネース|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anaximenes|qq=}} believed it to be "air." [[:en:Heraclitus|Heraclitus]] asserted that fire was the fundamental principle.<ref name='josetsu' /> These perspectives are sometimes regarded as forms of [[:en:Monism|Monism]].<ref name='josetsu' />
The French philosopher [[:en:Blaise Pascal|Blaise Pascal]] positioned humans between angels and beasts, viewing them as intermediaries between the supernatural and the natural world.<ref>''Pensées'' 358</ref><ref name='josetsu' />
== Natural Science == By the 18th century, the rise of [[:en:Natural science|Natural science]] led to a mode of inquiry that excluded subjective meaning and consciousness. Many scholars have noted this as a key characteristic of modern science.
Due to this exclusion, scientific conceptions of nature often appear impoverished. For example, ethical dimensions are absent from scientific understandings of nature.<ref name='josetsu' />
Some proponents of natural science dismiss natural philosophy as outdated.<ref name='josetsu' /> However, the unchecked application of scientific thinking has contributed to global issues such as [[:en:Environmental issues|Environmental problems]].<ref name='josetsu' /> Natural science lacks the means for deep ethical reflection and moral guidance.<ref name='josetsu' />
Since issues like environmental destruction are fundamentally ethical, they require philosophical rather than purely scientific approaches.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Personal Views === Some scientists and researchers in [[:en:Biology|Biology]], particularly [[:en:Ecology|Ecology]], use the term "shizenkan" to describe their personal perspectives on nature. For example, [[:en:Kinji Imanishi|Kinji Imanishi]] published ''Watashi no Shizenkan'' (My View of Nature) in 1978.
== Modern Natural Philosophy ==

The [[:en:Liezi|Liezi]], attributed to [[:en:Liezi|Liezi]], posited that everything initially existed in a chaotic state of [[:en:Qi|Qi]].<ref name='josetsu' />

=== Western Views === According to [[:en:Aristotle|Aristotle]], the first philosopher to engage in naturalistic inquiry was {{ill|Thales|ja|タレース|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thales|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タレス}}, who is considered the originator of "Why?" philosophy. Thales proposed that the fundamental principle, or {{ill|Arche|ja|アルケー|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arche|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, was water.<ref name='josetsu' />
[[:en:Anaximander|Anaximander]] proposed that the Arche was "the infinite (Apeiron)," while {{ill|Anaximenes|ja|アナクシメネース|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anaximenes|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} believed it to be "air." [[:en:Heraclitus|Heraclitus]] asserted that fire was the fundamental principle.<ref name='josetsu' /> These perspectives are sometimes regarded as forms of [[:en:Monism|Monism]].<ref name='josetsu' />
The French philosopher [[:en:Blaise Pascal|Blaise Pascal]] positioned humans between angels and beasts, viewing them as intermediaries between the supernatural and the natural world.<ref>''Pensées'' 358</ref><ref name='josetsu' />
== Natural Science == By the 18th century, the rise of [[:en:Natural science|Natural science]] led to a mode of inquiry that excluded subjective meaning and consciousness. Many scholars have noted this as a key characteristic of modern science.
Due to this exclusion, scientific conceptions of nature often appear impoverished. For example, ethical dimensions are absent from scientific understandings of nature.<ref name='josetsu' />
Some proponents of natural science dismiss natural philosophy as outdated.<ref name='josetsu' /> However, the unchecked application of scientific thinking has contributed to global issues such as [[:en:Environmental issues|Environmental problems]].<ref name='josetsu' /> Natural science lacks the means for deep ethical reflection and moral guidance.<ref name='josetsu' />
Since issues like environmental destruction are fundamentally ethical, they require philosophical rather than purely scientific approaches.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Personal Views === Some scientists and researchers in [[:en:Biology|Biology]], particularly [[:en:Ecology|Ecology]], use the term "shizenkan" to describe their personal perspectives on nature. For example, [[:en:Kinji Imanishi|Kinji Imanishi]] published ''Watashi no Shizenkan'' (My View of Nature) in 1978.
== Modern Natural Philosophy ==

revision.text:
'''Shizenkan''' (自然観) refers to the perception of [[:en:Nature|Nature]] as a fundamental value system. It is believed that there are significant differences depending on [[:en:Culture|Bunka]].<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Droz|first=Layna|last2=Chen|first2=Hsun-Mei|last3=Chu|first3=Hung-Tao|last4=Fajrini|first4=Rika|last5=Imbong|first5=Jerry|last6=Jannel|first6=Romaric|last7=Komatsubara|first7=Orika|last8=Lagasca-Hiloma|first8=Concordia Marie A.|last9=Meas|first9=Chansatya|date=2022-05-31|title=Exploring the diversity of conceptualizations of nature in East and South-East Asia|url=https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-022-01186-5|journal=Humanities and Social Sciences Communications|volume=9|issue=1|pages=1–12|language=en|doi=10.1057/s41599-022-01186-5|issn=2662-9992}}</ref>
== Mythological Views of Nature == Among the earliest conceptions of nature are those described in the mythologies of various societies, which illustrate the relationship between humans and nature.
A unique aspect of mythological perceptions of nature is that they also explain history. This is a strength that surpasses even [[:en:Natural philosophy|Philosophical Views of Nature]], which tend to avoid historical explanations.<ref name='josetsu'>{{Cite book|title=Shizen Tetsugaku Josetsu|author=Imamichi Tomonobu|publisher=Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|year=1993}}</ref>
In the {{ill|Judeo-Christian tradition|ja|ユダヤ教・キリスト教圏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Judeo-Christian tradition|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, references to nature can be found in the [[:en:Old Testament|Old Testament]] and [[:en:New Testament|New Testament]], while in the Islamic world, such references appear in the {{ill|Quran|ja|コーラン|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Quran|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クルアーン}}. In Japan, natural perceptions from the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Manyoshu]], compiled in ancient times, provide insight into the perspectives of that era.
In {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシャ神話|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greek mythology|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ギリシア神話}}, nature is depicted through various deities. The wind, for example, had different gods: the north wind was ruled by [[:en:Boreas (disambiguation)|Boreas]], and the west wind by {{ill|Zephyrus|ja|ゼフュリス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zephyrus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Similarly, fire was governed by its own deity, and even hearth fires had separate divine representations.
== Natural Philosophy == Natural philosophy seeks to discover logical structures and laws governing nature.<ref name='josetsu' /> This field of philosophy extends broadly and, in some contexts, encompasses the entirety of philosophical thought.
{{main|Natural philosophy}}
=== Eastern Views === In [[:en:Chinese philosophy|Chinese philosophy]], it was believed that "pure and light substances rise to form the heavens, while impure and heavy substances descend to form the earth." When applied to the classical [[:en:Big Four|Four Elements]], wind and fire were considered light and rising, while the earth element was regarded as heavy.<ref name='josetsu' />
The [[:en:Liezi|Liezi]], attributed to [[:en:Liezi|Liezi]], posited that everything initially existed in a chaotic state of [[:en:Qi|Qi]].<ref name='josetsu' />
China also developed the concept of [[:en:Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|Five Elements (Wuxing)]], which consists of wood, fire, earth, metal, and water.<ref name='josetsu' /> This theory, rather than defining elements in a material sense, is often interpreted as representing dynamic phenomena.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Western Views === According to [[:en:Aristotle|Aristotle]], the first philosopher to engage in naturalistic inquiry was {{ill|Thales|ja|タレース|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thales|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タレス}}, who is considered the originator of "Why?" philosophy. Thales proposed that the fundamental principle, or {{ill|Arche|ja|アルケー|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arche|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, was water.<ref name='josetsu' />
[[:en:Anaximander|Anaximander]] proposed that the Arche was "the infinite (Apeiron)," while {{ill|Anaximenes|ja|アナクシメネース|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anaximenes|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} believed it to be "air." [[:en:Heraclitus|Heraclitus]] asserted that fire was the fundamental principle.<ref name='josetsu' /> These perspectives are sometimes regarded as forms of [[:en:Monism|Monism]].<ref name='josetsu' />
The French philosopher [[:en:Blaise Pascal|Blaise Pascal]] positioned humans between angels and beasts, viewing them as intermediaries between the supernatural and the natural world.<ref>''Pensées'' 358</ref><ref name='josetsu' />
== Natural Science == By the 18th century, the rise of [[:en:Natural science|Natural science]] led to a mode of inquiry that excluded subjective meaning and consciousness. Many scholars have noted this as a key characteristic of modern science.
Due to this exclusion, scientific conceptions of nature often appear impoverished. For example, ethical dimensions are absent from scientific understandings of nature.<ref name='josetsu' />
Some proponents of natural science dismiss natural philosophy as outdated.<ref name='josetsu' /> However, the unchecked application of scientific thinking has contributed to global issues such as [[:en:Environmental issues|Environmental problems]].<ref name='josetsu' /> Natural science lacks the means for deep ethical reflection and moral guidance.<ref name='josetsu' />
Since issues like environmental destruction are fundamentally ethical, they require philosophical rather than purely scientific approaches.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Personal Views === Some scientists and researchers in [[:en:Biology|Biology]], particularly [[:en:Ecology|Ecology]], use the term "shizenkan" to describe their personal perspectives on nature. For example, [[:en:Kinji Imanishi|Kinji Imanishi]] published ''Watashi no Shizenkan'' (My View of Nature) in 1978.

[[:en:Heraclitus|Heraclitus]] asserted that fire was the fundamental principle.<ref name='josetsu' /> These perspectives are sometimes regarded as forms of [[:en:Monism|Monism]].<ref name='josetsu' />
The French philosopher [[:en:Blaise Pascal|Blaise Pascal]] positioned humans between angels and beasts, viewing them as intermediaries between the supernatural and the natural world.<ref>''Pensées'' 358</ref><ref name='josetsu' />
== Natural Science == By the 18th century, the rise of [[:en:Natural science|Natural science]] led to a mode of inquiry that excluded subjective meaning and consciousness. Many scholars have noted this as a key characteristic of modern science.
Due to this exclusion, scientific conceptions of nature often appear impoverished. For example, ethical dimensions are absent from scientific understandings of nature.<ref name='josetsu' />
Some proponents of natural science dismiss natural philosophy as outdated.<ref name='josetsu' /> However, the unchecked application of scientific thinking has contributed to global issues such as [[:en:Environmental issues|Environmental problems]].<ref name='josetsu' /> Natural science lacks the means for deep ethical reflection and moral guidance.<ref name='josetsu' />
Since issues like environmental destruction are fundamentally ethical, they require philosophical rather than purely scientific approaches.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Personal Views === Some scientists and researchers in [[:en:Biology|Biology]], particularly [[:en:Ecology|Ecology]], use the term "shizenkan" to describe their personal perspectives on nature. For example, [[:en:Kinji Imanishi|Kinji Imanishi]] published ''Watashi no Shizenkan'' (My View of Nature) in 1978.
== Modern Natural Philosophy ==

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shizenkan''' (自然観) refers to the perception of [[:en:Nature|Nature]] as a fundamental value system. It is believed that there are significant differences depending on [[:en:Culture|Bunka]].<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Droz|first=Layna|last2=Chen|first2=Hsun-Mei|last3=Chu|first3=Hung-Tao|last4=Fajrini|first4=Rika|last5=Imbong|first5=Jerry|last6=Jannel|first6=Romaric|last7=Komatsubara|first7=Orika|last8=Lagasca-Hiloma|first8=Concordia Marie A.|last9=Meas|first9=Chansatya|date=2022-05-31|title=Exploring the diversity of conceptualizations of nature in East and South-East Asia|url=https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-022-01186-5|journal=Humanities and Social Sciences Communications|volume=9|issue=1|pages=1–12|language=en|doi=10.1057/s41599-022-01186-5|issn=2662-9992}}</ref>
== Mythological Views of Nature == Among the earliest conceptions of nature are those described in the mythologies of various societies, which illustrate the relationship between humans and nature.
A unique aspect of mythological perceptions of nature is that they also explain history. This is a strength that surpasses even [[:en:Natural philosophy|Philosophical Views of Nature]], which tend to avoid historical explanations.<ref name='josetsu'>{{Cite book|title=Shizen Tetsugaku Josetsu|author=Imamichi Tomonobu|publisher=Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|year=1993}}</ref>
In the {{ill|Judeo-Christian tradition|ja|ユダヤ教・キリスト教圏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Judeo-Christian tradition|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, references to nature can be found in the [[:en:Old Testament|Old Testament]] and [[:en:New Testament|New Testament]], while in the Islamic world, such references appear in the {{ill|Quran|ja|コーラン|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Quran|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クルアーン}}. In Japan, natural perceptions from the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Manyoshu]], compiled in ancient times, provide insight into the perspectives of that era.
In {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシャ神話|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greek mythology|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ギリシア神話}}, nature is depicted through various deities. The wind, for example, had different gods: the north wind was ruled by [[:en:Boreas (disambiguation)|Boreas]], and the west wind by {{ill|Zephyrus|ja|ゼフュリス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zephyrus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Similarly, fire was governed by its own deity, and even hearth fires had separate divine representations.
== Natural Philosophy == Natural philosophy seeks to discover logical structures and laws governing nature.<ref name='josetsu' /> This field of philosophy extends broadly and, in some contexts, encompasses the entirety of philosophical thought.
{{main|Natural philosophy}}
=== Eastern Views === In [[:en:Chinese philosophy|Chinese philosophy]], it was believed that "pure and light substances rise to form the heavens, while impure and heavy substances descend to form the earth." When applied to the classical [[:en:Big Four|Four Elements]], wind and fire were considered light and rising, while the earth element was regarded as heavy.<ref name='josetsu' />
The [[:en:Liezi|Liezi]], attributed to [[:en:Liezi|Liezi]], posited that everything initially existed in a chaotic state of [[:en:Qi|Qi]].<ref name='josetsu' />
China also developed the concept of [[:en:Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|Five Elements (Wuxing)]], which consists of wood, fire, earth, metal, and water.<ref name='josetsu' /> This theory, rather than defining elements in a material sense, is often interpreted as representing dynamic phenomena.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Western Views === According to [[:en:Aristotle|Aristotle]], the first philosopher to engage in naturalistic inquiry was {{ill|Thales|ja|タレース|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thales|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タレス}}, who is considered the originator of "Why?" philosophy. Thales proposed that the fundamental principle, or {{ill|Arche|ja|アルケー|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arche|qq=|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}, was water.<ref name='josetsu' />
[[:en:Anaximander|Anaximander]] proposed that the Arche was "the infinite (Apeiron)," while {{ill|Anaximenes|ja|アナクシメネース|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anaximenes|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} believed it to be "air." [[:en:Heraclitus|Heraclitus]] asserted that fire was the fundamental principle.<ref name='josetsu' /> These perspectives are sometimes regarded as forms of [[:en:Monism|Monism]].<ref name='josetsu' />
The French philosopher [[:en:Blaise Pascal|Blaise Pascal]] positioned humans between angels and beasts, viewing them as intermediaries between the supernatural and the natural world.<ref>''Pensées'' 358</ref><ref name='josetsu' />
== Natural Science == By the 18th century, the rise of [[:en:Natural science|Natural science]] led to a mode of inquiry that excluded subjective meaning and consciousness. Many scholars have noted this as a key characteristic of modern science.
Due to this exclusion, scientific conceptions of nature often appear impoverished. For example, ethical dimensions are absent from scientific understandings of nature.<ref name='josetsu' />
Some proponents of natural science dismiss natural philosophy as outdated.<ref name='josetsu' /> However, the unchecked application of scientific thinking has contributed to global issues such as [[:en:Environmental issues|Environmental problems]].<ref name='josetsu' /> Natural science lacks the means for deep ethical reflection and moral guidance.<ref name='josetsu' />
Since issues like environmental destruction are fundamentally ethical, they require philosophical rather than purely scientific approaches.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Personal Views === Some scientists and researchers in [[:en:Biology|Biology]], particularly [[:en:Ecology|Ecology]], use the term "shizenkan" to describe their personal perspectives on nature. For example, [[:en:Kinji Imanishi|Kinji Imanishi]] published ''Watashi no Shizenkan'' (My View of Nature) in 1978.
== Modern Natural Philosophy ==

China also developed the concept of [[:en:Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|Five Elements (Wuxing)]], which consists of wood, fire, earth, metal, and water.<ref name='josetsu' /> This theory, rather than defining elements in a material sense, is often interpreted as representing dynamic phenomena.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Western Views === According to [[:en:Aristotle|Aristotle]], the first philosopher to engage in naturalistic inquiry was {{ill|Thales|ja|タレース|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thales|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タレス|1=Thales|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q36303}}, who is considered the originator of "Why?" philosophy. Thales proposed that the fundamental principle, or {{ill|Arche|ja|アルケー|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arche|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=arche|WD=Q196638}}, was water.<ref name='josetsu' />
[[:en:Anaximander|Anaximander]] proposed that the Arche was "the infinite (Apeiron)," while {{ill|Anaximenes|ja|アナクシメネース|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anaximenes|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} believed it to be "air." [[:en:Heraclitus|Heraclitus]] asserted that fire was the fundamental principle.<ref name='josetsu' /> These perspectives are sometimes regarded as forms of [[:en:Monism|Monism]].<ref name='josetsu' />
The French philosopher [[:en:Blaise Pascal|Blaise Pascal]] positioned humans between angels and beasts, viewing them as intermediaries between the supernatural and the natural world.<ref>''Pensées'' 358</ref><ref name='josetsu' />
== Natural Science == By the 18th century, the rise of [[:en:Natural science|Natural science]] led to a mode of inquiry that excluded subjective meaning and consciousness. Many scholars have noted this as a key characteristic of modern science.
Due to this exclusion, scientific conceptions of nature often appear impoverished. For example, ethical dimensions are absent from scientific understandings of nature.<ref name='josetsu' />
Some proponents of natural science dismiss natural philosophy as outdated.<ref name='josetsu' /> However, the unchecked application of scientific thinking has contributed to global issues such as [[:en:Environmental issues|Environmental problems]].<ref name='josetsu' /> Natural science lacks the means for deep ethical reflection and moral guidance.<ref name='josetsu' />
Since issues like environmental destruction are fundamentally ethical, they require philosophical rather than purely scientific approaches.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Personal Views === Some scientists and researchers in [[:en:Biology|Biology]], particularly [[:en:Ecology|Ecology]], use the term "shizenkan" to describe their personal perspectives on nature. For example, [[:en:Kinji Imanishi|Kinji Imanishi]] published ''Watashi no Shizenkan'' (My View of Nature) in 1978.
== Modern Natural Philosophy ==

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11612878]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:22, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shizenkan''' (自然観) refers to the perception of [[:en:Nature|Nature]] as a fundamental value system. It is believed that there are significant differences depending on [[:en:Culture|Bunka]].<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Droz|first=Layna|last2=Chen|first2=Hsun-Mei|last3=Chu|first3=Hung-Tao|last4=Fajrini|first4=Rika|last5=Imbong|first5=Jerry|last6=Jannel|first6=Romaric|last7=Komatsubara|first7=Orika|last8=Lagasca-Hiloma|first8=Concordia Marie A.|last9=Meas|first9=Chansatya|date=2022-05-31|title=Exploring the diversity of conceptualizations of nature in East and South-East Asia|url=https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-022-01186-5|journal=Humanities and Social Sciences Communications|volume=9|issue=1|pages=1–12|language=en|doi=10.1057/s41599-022-01186-5|issn=2662-9992}}</ref>
== Mythological Views of Nature == Among the earliest conceptions of nature are those described in the mythologies of various societies, which illustrate the relationship between humans and nature.
A unique aspect of mythological perceptions of nature is that they also explain history. This is a strength that surpasses even [[:en:Natural philosophy|Philosophical Views of Nature]], which tend to avoid historical explanations.<ref name='josetsu'>{{Cite book|title=Shizen Tetsugaku Josetsu|author=Imamichi Tomonobu|publisher=Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|year=1993}}</ref>
In the {{ill|Judeo-Christian tradition|ja|ユダヤ教・キリスト教圏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Judeo-Christian tradition|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, references to nature can be found in the [[:en:Old Testament|Old Testament]] and [[:en:New Testament|New Testament]], while in the Islamic world, such references appear in the {{ill|Quran|ja|コーラン|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Quran|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クルアーン|1=Qur’an|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q428}}. In Japan, natural perceptions from the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Manyoshu]], compiled in ancient times, provide insight into the perspectives of that era.
In {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシャ神話|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greek mythology|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ギリシア神話|1=Greek mythology|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q34726}}, nature is depicted through various deities. The wind, for example, had different gods: the north wind was ruled by [[:en:Boreas (disambiguation)|Boreas]], and the west wind by {{ill|Zephyrus|ja|ゼフュリス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zephyrus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Similarly, fire was governed by its own deity, and even hearth fires had separate divine representations.
== Natural Philosophy == Natural philosophy seeks to discover logical structures and laws governing nature.<ref name='josetsu' /> This field of philosophy extends broadly and, in some contexts, encompasses the entirety of philosophical thought.
{{main|Natural philosophy}}
=== Eastern Views === In [[:en:Chinese philosophy|Chinese philosophy]], it was believed that "pure and light substances rise to form the heavens, while impure and heavy substances descend to form the earth." When applied to the classical [[:en:Big Four|Four Elements]], wind and fire were considered light and rising, while the earth element was regarded as heavy.<ref name='josetsu' />
The [[:en:Liezi|Liezi]], attributed to [[:en:Liezi|Liezi]], posited that everything initially existed in a chaotic state of [[:en:Qi|Qi]].<ref name='josetsu' />
China also developed the concept of [[:en:Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|Five Elements (Wuxing)]], which consists of wood, fire, earth, metal, and water.<ref name='josetsu' /> This theory, rather than defining elements in a material sense, is often interpreted as representing dynamic phenomena.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Western Views === According to [[:en:Aristotle|Aristotle]], the first philosopher to engage in naturalistic inquiry was {{ill|Thales|ja|タレース|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thales|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タレス|1=Thales|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q36303}}, who is considered the originator of "Why?" philosophy. Thales proposed that the fundamental principle, or {{ill|Arche|ja|アルケー|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arche|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=arche|WD=Q196638}}, was water.<ref name='josetsu' />
[[:en:Anaximander|Anaximander]] proposed that the Arche was "the infinite (Apeiron)," while {{ill|Anaximenes|ja|アナクシメネース|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anaximenes|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} believed it to be "air." [[:en:Heraclitus|Heraclitus]] asserted that fire was the fundamental principle.<ref name='josetsu' /> These perspectives are sometimes regarded as forms of [[:en:Monism|Monism]].<ref name='josetsu' />
The French philosopher [[:en:Blaise Pascal|Blaise Pascal]] positioned humans between angels and beasts, viewing them as intermediaries between the supernatural and the natural world.<ref>''Pensées'' 358</ref><ref name='josetsu' />
== Natural Science == By the 18th century, the rise of [[:en:Natural science|Natural science]] led to a mode of inquiry that excluded subjective meaning and consciousness. Many scholars have noted this as a key characteristic of modern science.
Due to this exclusion, scientific conceptions of nature often appear impoverished. For example, ethical dimensions are absent from scientific understandings of nature.<ref name='josetsu' />
Some proponents of natural science dismiss natural philosophy as outdated.<ref name='josetsu' /> However, the unchecked application of scientific thinking has contributed to global issues such as [[:en:Environmental issues|Environmental problems]].<ref name='josetsu' /> Natural science lacks the means for deep ethical reflection and moral guidance.<ref name='josetsu' />
Since issues like environmental destruction are fundamentally ethical, they require philosophical rather than purely scientific approaches.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Personal Views === Some scientists and researchers in [[:en:Biology|Biology]], particularly [[:en:Ecology|Ecology]], use the term "shizenkan" to describe their personal perspectives on nature. For example, [[:en:Kinji Imanishi|Kinji Imanishi]] published ''Watashi no Shizenkan'' (My View of Nature) in 1978.

== Natural Philosophy == Natural philosophy seeks to discover logical structures and laws governing nature.<ref name='josetsu' /> This field of philosophy extends broadly and, in some contexts, encompasses the entirety of philosophical thought.
{{main|Natural philosophy}}
=== Eastern Views === In [[:en:Chinese philosophy|Chinese philosophy]], it was believed that "pure and light substances rise to form the heavens, while impure and heavy substances descend to form the earth." When applied to the classical [[:en:Big Four|Four Elements]], wind and fire were considered light and rising, while the earth element was regarded as heavy.<ref name='josetsu' />
The [[:en:Liezi|Liezi]], attributed to [[:en:Liezi|Liezi]], posited that everything initially existed in a chaotic state of [[:en:Qi|Qi]].<ref name='josetsu' />
China also developed the concept of [[:en:Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|Five Elements (Wuxing)]], which consists of wood, fire, earth, metal, and water.<ref name='josetsu' /> This theory, rather than defining elements in a material sense, is often interpreted as representing dynamic phenomena.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Western Views === According to [[:en:Aristotle|Aristotle]], the first philosopher to engage in naturalistic inquiry was {{ill|Thales|ja|タレース|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thales|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タレス|1=Thales|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q36303}}, who is considered the originator of "Why?" philosophy. Thales proposed that the fundamental principle, or {{ill|Arche|ja|アルケー|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arche|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=arche|WD=Q196638}}, was water.<ref name='josetsu' />
[[:en:Anaximander|Anaximander]] proposed that the Arche was "the infinite (Apeiron)," while {{ill|Anaximenes|ja|アナクシメネース|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anaximenes|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} believed it to be "air." [[:en:Heraclitus|Heraclitus]] asserted that fire was the fundamental principle.<ref name='josetsu' /> These perspectives are sometimes regarded as forms of [[:en:Monism|Monism]].<ref name='josetsu' />
The French philosopher [[:en:Blaise Pascal|Blaise Pascal]] positioned humans between angels and beasts, viewing them as intermediaries between the supernatural and the natural world.<ref>''Pensées'' 358</ref><ref name='josetsu' />
== Natural Science == By the 18th century, the rise of [[:en:Natural science|Natural science]] led to a mode of inquiry that excluded subjective meaning and consciousness. Many scholars have noted this as a key characteristic of modern science.
Due to this exclusion, scientific conceptions of nature often appear impoverished. For example, ethical dimensions are absent from scientific understandings of nature.<ref name='josetsu' />
Some proponents of natural science dismiss natural philosophy as outdated.<ref name='josetsu' /> However, the unchecked application of scientific thinking has contributed to global issues such as [[:en:Environmental issues|Environmental problems]].<ref name='josetsu' /> Natural science lacks the means for deep ethical reflection and moral guidance.<ref name='josetsu' />
Since issues like environmental destruction are fundamentally ethical, they require philosophical rather than purely scientific approaches.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Personal Views === Some scientists and researchers in [[:en:Biology|Biology]], particularly [[:en:Ecology|Ecology]], use the term "shizenkan" to describe their personal perspectives on nature. For example, [[:en:Kinji Imanishi|Kinji Imanishi]] published ''Watashi no Shizenkan'' (My View of Nature) in 1978.

== Mythological Views of Nature == Among the earliest conceptions of nature are those described in the mythologies of various societies, which illustrate the relationship between humans and nature.
A unique aspect of mythological perceptions of nature is that they also explain history. This is a strength that surpasses even [[:en:Natural philosophy|Philosophical Views of Nature]], which tend to avoid historical explanations.<ref name='josetsu'>{{Cite book|title=Shizen Tetsugaku Josetsu|author=Imamichi Tomonobu|publisher=Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|year=1993}}</ref>

In Japan, natural perceptions from the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Manyoshu]], compiled in ancient times, provide insight into the perspectives of that era.
In {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシャ神話|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greek mythology|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ギリシア神話|1=Greek mythology|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q34726}}, nature is depicted through various deities. The wind, for example, had different gods: the north wind was ruled by [[:en:Boreas (disambiguation)|Boreas]], and the west wind by {{ill|Zephyrus|ja|ゼフュリス|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zephyrus|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Similarly, fire was governed by its own deity, and even hearth fires had separate divine representations.

In {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシャ神話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greek mythology|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ギリシア神話|1=Greek mythology|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q34726}}, nature is depicted through various deities. The wind, for example, had different gods: the north wind was ruled by [[:en:Boreas (disambiguation)|Boreas]], and the west wind by {{ill|Zephyrus|ja|ゼフュリス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zephyrus|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Similarly, fire was governed by its own deity, and even hearth fires had separate divine representations.
== Natural Philosophy == Natural philosophy seeks to discover logical structures and laws governing nature.<ref name='josetsu' /> This field of philosophy extends broadly and, in some contexts, encompasses the entirety of philosophical thought.
{{main|Natural philosophy}}
=== Eastern Views === In [[:en:Chinese philosophy|Chinese philosophy]], it was believed that "pure and light substances rise to form the heavens, while impure and heavy substances descend to form the earth." When applied to the classical [[:en:Big Four|Four Elements]], wind and fire were considered light and rising, while the earth element was regarded as heavy.<ref name='josetsu' />
The [[:en:Liezi|Liezi]], attributed to [[:en:Liezi|Liezi]], posited that everything initially existed in a chaotic state of [[:en:Qi|Qi]].<ref name='josetsu' />
China also developed the concept of [[:en:Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|Five Elements (Wuxing)]], which consists of wood, fire, earth, metal, and water.<ref name='josetsu' /> This theory, rather than defining elements in a material sense, is often interpreted as representing dynamic phenomena.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Western Views === According to [[:en:Aristotle|Aristotle]], the first philosopher to engage in naturalistic inquiry was {{ill|Thales|ja|タレース|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thales|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タレス|1=Thales|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q36303}}, who is considered the originator of "Why?" philosophy. Thales proposed that the fundamental principle, or {{ill|Arche|ja|アルケー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arche|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=arche|WD=Q196638}}, was water.<ref name='josetsu' />
[[:en:Anaximander|Anaximander]] proposed that the Arche was "the infinite (Apeiron)," while {{ill|Anaximenes|ja|アナクシメネース|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anaximenes|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} believed it to be "air." [[:en:Heraclitus|Heraclitus]] asserted that fire was the fundamental principle.<ref name='josetsu' /> These perspectives are sometimes regarded as forms of [[:en:Monism|Monism]].<ref name='josetsu' />
The French philosopher [[:en:Blaise Pascal|Blaise Pascal]] positioned humans between angels and beasts, viewing them as intermediaries between the supernatural and the natural world.<ref>''Pensées'' 358</ref><ref name='josetsu' />
== Natural Science == By the 18th century, the rise of [[:en:Natural science|Natural science]] led to a mode of inquiry that excluded subjective meaning and consciousness. Many scholars have noted this as a key characteristic of modern science.
Due to this exclusion, scientific conceptions of nature often appear impoverished. For example, ethical dimensions are absent from scientific understandings of nature.<ref name='josetsu' />
Some proponents of natural science dismiss natural philosophy as outdated.<ref name='josetsu' /> However, the unchecked application of scientific thinking has contributed to global issues such as [[:en:Environmental issues|Environmental problems]].<ref name='josetsu' /> Natural science lacks the means for deep ethical reflection and moral guidance.<ref name='josetsu' />
Since issues like environmental destruction are fundamentally ethical, they require philosophical rather than purely scientific approaches.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Personal Views === Some scientists and researchers in [[:en:Biology|Biology]], particularly [[:en:Ecology|Ecology]], use the term "shizenkan" to describe their personal perspectives on nature. For example, [[:en:Kinji Imanishi|Kinji Imanishi]] published ''Watashi no Shizenkan'' (My View of Nature) in 1978.
== Modern Natural Philosophy ==

It is believed that there are significant differences depending on [[:en:Culture|Bunka]].<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Droz|first=Layna|last2=Chen|first2=Hsun-Mei|last3=Chu|first3=Hung-Tao|last4=Fajrini|first4=Rika|last5=Imbong|first5=Jerry|last6=Jannel|first6=Romaric|last7=Komatsubara|first7=Orika|last8=Lagasca-Hiloma|first8=Concordia Marie A.|last9=Meas|first9=Chansatya|date=2022-05-31|title=Exploring the diversity of conceptualizations of nature in East and South-East Asia|url=https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-022-01186-5|journal=Humanities and Social Sciences Communications|volume=9|issue=1|pages=1–12|language=en|doi=10.1057/s41599-022-01186-5|issn=2662-9992}}</ref>
== Mythological Views of Nature == Among the earliest conceptions of nature are those described in the mythologies of various societies, which illustrate the relationship between humans and nature.

Similarly, fire was governed by its own deity, and even hearth fires had separate divine representations.

When applied to the classical [[:en:Big Four|Four Elements]], wind and fire were considered light and rising, while the earth element was regarded as heavy.<ref name='josetsu' />
The [[:en:Liezi|Liezi]], attributed to [[:en:Liezi|Liezi]], posited that everything initially existed in a chaotic state of [[:en:Qi|Qi]].<ref name='josetsu' />
China also developed the concept of [[:en:Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|Five Elements (Wuxing)]], which consists of wood, fire, earth, metal, and water.<ref name='josetsu' /> This theory, rather than defining elements in a material sense, is often interpreted as representing dynamic phenomena.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Western Views === According to [[:en:Aristotle|Aristotle]], the first philosopher to engage in naturalistic inquiry was {{ill|Thales|ja|タレース|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thales|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タレス|1=Thales|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q36303}}, who is considered the originator of "Why?" philosophy. Thales proposed that the fundamental principle, or {{ill|Arche|ja|アルケー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arche|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=arche|WD=Q196638}}, was water.<ref name='josetsu' />

[[:en:Heraclitus|Heraclitus]] asserted that fire was the fundamental principle.<ref name='josetsu' /> These perspectives are sometimes regarded as forms of [[:en:Monism|Monism]].<ref name='josetsu' />
The French philosopher [[:en:Blaise Pascal|Blaise Pascal]] positioned humans between angels and beasts, viewing them as intermediaries between the supernatural and the natural world.<ref>''Pensées'' 358</ref><ref name='josetsu' />
== Natural Science == By the 18th century, the rise of [[:en:Natural science|Natural science]] led to a mode of inquiry that excluded subjective meaning and consciousness. Many scholars have noted this as a key characteristic of modern science.
Due to this exclusion, scientific conceptions of nature often appear impoverished. For example, ethical dimensions are absent from scientific understandings of nature.<ref name='josetsu' />
Some proponents of natural science dismiss natural philosophy as outdated.<ref name='josetsu' /> However, the unchecked application of scientific thinking has contributed to global issues such as [[:en:Environmental issues|Environmental problems]].<ref name='josetsu' /> Natural science lacks the means for deep ethical reflection and moral guidance.<ref name='josetsu' />
Since issues like environmental destruction are fundamentally ethical, they require philosophical rather than purely scientific approaches.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Personal Views === Some scientists and researchers in [[:en:Biology|Biology]], particularly [[:en:Ecology|Ecology]], use the term "shizenkan" to describe their personal perspectives on nature. == Mythological Views of Nature == Among the earliest conceptions of nature are those described in the mythologies of various societies, which illustrate the relationship between humans and nature.
A unique aspect of mythological perceptions of nature is that they also explain history. This is a strength that surpasses even [[:en:Natural philosophy|Philosophical Views of Nature]], which tend to avoid historical explanations.<ref name='josetsu'>{{Cite book|title=Shizen Tetsugaku Josetsu|author=Imamichi Tomonobu|publisher=Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|year=1993}}</ref>
In the {{ill|Judeo-Christian tradition|ja|ユダヤ教・キリスト教圏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Judeo-Christian tradition|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, references to nature can be found in the [[:en:Old Testament|Old Testament]] and [[:en:New Testament|New Testament]], while in the Islamic world, such references appear in the {{ill|Quran|ja|コーラン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Quran|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クルアーン|1=Qur’an|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q428}}. In Japan, natural perceptions from the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Manyoshu]], compiled in ancient times, provide insight into the perspectives of that era.
In {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシャ神話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greek mythology|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ギリシア神話|1=Greek mythology|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q34726}}, nature is depicted through various deities. The wind, for example, had different gods: the north wind was ruled by [[:en:Boreas (disambiguation)|Boreas]], and the west wind by {{ill|Zephyrus|ja|ゼフュリス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zephyrus|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Similarly, fire was governed by its own deity, and even hearth fires had separate divine representations.
== Natural Philosophy == Natural philosophy seeks to discover logical structures and laws governing nature.<ref name='josetsu' /> This field of philosophy extends broadly and, in some contexts, encompasses the entirety of philosophical thought.
{{main|Natural philosophy}}
=== Eastern Views === In [[:en:Chinese philosophy|Chinese philosophy]], it was believed that "pure and light substances rise to form the heavens, while impure and heavy substances descend to form the earth." When applied to the classical [[:en:Big Four|Four Elements]], wind and fire were considered light and rising, while the earth element was regarded as heavy.<ref name='josetsu' />
The [[:en:Liezi|Liezi]], attributed to [[:en:Liezi|Liezi]], posited that everything initially existed in a chaotic state of [[:en:Qi|Qi]].<ref name='josetsu' />
China also developed the concept of [[:en:Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|Five Elements (Wuxing)]], which consists of wood, fire, earth, metal, and water.<ref name='josetsu' /> This theory, rather than defining elements in a material sense, is often interpreted as representing dynamic phenomena.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Western Views === According to [[:en:Aristotle|Aristotle]], the first philosopher to engage in naturalistic inquiry was {{ill|Thales|ja|タレース|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thales|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タレス|1=Thales|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q36303}}, who is considered the originator of "Why?" philosophy. Thales proposed that the fundamental principle, or {{ill|Arche|ja|アルケー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arche|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=arche|WD=Q196638}}, was water.<ref name='josetsu' />
[[:en:Anaximander|Anaximander]] proposed that the Arche was "the infinite (Apeiron)," while {{ill|Anaximenes|ja|アナクシメネース|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anaximenes|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} believed it to be "air." [[:en:Heraclitus|Heraclitus]] asserted that fire was the fundamental principle.<ref name='josetsu' /> These perspectives are sometimes regarded as forms of [[:en:Monism|Monism]].<ref name='josetsu' />
The French philosopher [[:en:Blaise Pascal|Blaise Pascal]] positioned humans between angels and beasts, viewing them as intermediaries between the supernatural and the natural world.<ref>''Pensées'' 358</ref><ref name='josetsu' />
== Natural Science == By the 18th century, the rise of [[:en:Natural science|Natural science]] led to a mode of inquiry that excluded subjective meaning and consciousness. Many scholars have noted this as a key characteristic of modern science.
Due to this exclusion, scientific conceptions of nature often appear impoverished. For example, ethical dimensions are absent from scientific understandings of nature.<ref name='josetsu' />
Some proponents of natural science dismiss natural philosophy as outdated.<ref name='josetsu' /> However, the unchecked application of scientific thinking has contributed to global issues such as [[:en:Environmental issues|Environmental problems]].<ref name='josetsu' /> Natural science lacks the means for deep ethical reflection and moral guidance.<ref name='josetsu' />
Since issues like environmental destruction are fundamentally ethical, they require philosophical rather than purely scientific approaches.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Personal Views === Some scientists and researchers in [[:en:Biology|Biology]], particularly [[:en:Ecology|Ecology]], use the term "shizenkan" to describe their personal perspectives on nature. For example, [[:en:Kinji Imanishi|Kinji Imanishi]] published ''Watashi no Shizenkan'' (My View of Nature) in 1978.
== Modern Natural Philosophy ==

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:自然観]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11612878]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:22, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shizenkan''' (自然観) refers to the perception of [[:en:Nature|Nature]] as a fundamental value system. It is believed that there are significant differences depending on [[:en:Culture|Bunka]].<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Droz|first=Layna|last2=Chen|first2=Hsun-Mei|last3=Chu|first3=Hung-Tao|last4=Fajrini|first4=Rika|last5=Imbong|first5=Jerry|last6=Jannel|first6=Romaric|last7=Komatsubara|first7=Orika|last8=Lagasca-Hiloma|first8=Concordia Marie A.|last9=Meas|first9=Chansatya|date=2022-05-31|title=Exploring the diversity of conceptualizations of nature in East and South-East Asia|url=https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-022-01186-5|journal=Humanities and Social Sciences Communications|volume=9|issue=1|pages=1–12|language=en|doi=10.1057/s41599-022-01186-5|issn=2662-9992}}</ref>
== Mythological Views of Nature == Among the earliest conceptions of nature are those described in the mythologies of various societies, which illustrate the relationship between humans and nature.
A unique aspect of mythological perceptions of nature is that they also explain history. This is a strength that surpasses even [[:en:Natural philosophy|Philosophical Views of Nature]], which tend to avoid historical explanations.<ref name='josetsu'>{{Cite book|title=Shizen Tetsugaku Josetsu|author=Imamichi Tomonobu|publisher=Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|year=1993}}</ref>
In the {{ill|Judeo-Christian tradition|ja|ユダヤ教・キリスト教圏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Judeo-Christian tradition|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, references to nature can be found in the [[:en:Old Testament|Old Testament]] and [[:en:New Testament|New Testament]], while in the Islamic world, such references appear in the {{ill|Quran|ja|コーラン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Quran|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クルアーン|1=Qur’an|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q428}}. In Japan, natural perceptions from the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Manyoshu]], compiled in ancient times, provide insight into the perspectives of that era.
In {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシャ神話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greek mythology|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ギリシア神話|1=Greek mythology|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q34726}}, nature is depicted through various deities. The wind, for example, had different gods: the north wind was ruled by [[:en:Boreas (disambiguation)|Boreas]], and the west wind by {{ill|Zephyrus|ja|ゼフュリス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zephyrus|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Similarly, fire was governed by its own deity, and even hearth fires had separate divine representations.
== Natural Philosophy == Natural philosophy seeks to discover logical structures and laws governing nature.<ref name='josetsu' /> This field of philosophy extends broadly and, in some contexts, encompasses the entirety of philosophical thought.
{{main|Natural philosophy}}
=== Eastern Views === In [[:en:Chinese philosophy|Chinese philosophy]], it was believed that "pure and light substances rise to form the heavens, while impure and heavy substances descend to form the earth." When applied to the classical [[:en:Big Four|Four Elements]], wind and fire were considered light and rising, while the earth element was regarded as heavy.<ref name='josetsu' />
The [[:en:Liezi|Liezi]], attributed to [[:en:Liezi|Liezi]], posited that everything initially existed in a chaotic state of [[:en:Qi|Qi]].<ref name='josetsu' />
China also developed the concept of [[:en:Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|Five Elements (Wuxing)]], which consists of wood, fire, earth, metal, and water.<ref name='josetsu' /> This theory, rather than defining elements in a material sense, is often interpreted as representing dynamic phenomena.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Western Views === According to [[:en:Aristotle|Aristotle]], the first philosopher to engage in naturalistic inquiry was {{ill|Thales|ja|タレース|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thales|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タレス|1=Thales|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q36303}}, who is considered the originator of "Why?" philosophy. Thales proposed that the fundamental principle, or {{ill|Arche|ja|アルケー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arche|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=arche|WD=Q196638}}, was water.<ref name='josetsu' />
[[:en:Anaximander|Anaximander]] proposed that the Arche was "the infinite (Apeiron)," while {{ill|Anaximenes|ja|アナクシメネース|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anaximenes|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} believed it to be "air." [[:en:Heraclitus|Heraclitus]] asserted that fire was the fundamental principle.<ref name='josetsu' /> These perspectives are sometimes regarded as forms of [[:en:Monism|Monism]].<ref name='josetsu' />
The French philosopher [[:en:Blaise Pascal|Blaise Pascal]] positioned humans between angels and beasts, viewing them as intermediaries between the supernatural and the natural world.<ref>''Pensées'' 358</ref><ref name='josetsu' />
== Natural Science == By the 18th century, the rise of [[:en:Natural science|Natural science]] led to a mode of inquiry that excluded subjective meaning and consciousness. Many scholars have noted this as a key characteristic of modern science.
Due to this exclusion, scientific conceptions of nature often appear impoverished. For example, ethical dimensions are absent from scientific understandings of nature.<ref name='josetsu' />
Some proponents of natural science dismiss natural philosophy as outdated.<ref name='josetsu' /> However, the unchecked application of scientific thinking has contributed to global issues such as [[:en:Environmental issues|Environmental problems]].<ref name='josetsu' /> Natural science lacks the means for deep ethical reflection and moral guidance.<ref name='josetsu' />
Since issues like environmental destruction are fundamentally ethical, they require philosophical rather than purely scientific approaches.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Personal Views === Some scientists and researchers in [[:en:Biology|Biology]], particularly [[:en:Ecology|Ecology]], use the term "shizenkan" to describe their personal perspectives on nature. For example, [[:en:Kinji Imanishi|Kinji Imanishi]] published ''Watashi no Shizenkan'' (My View of Nature) in 1978.
== Modern Natural Philosophy ==
== References == <references/>

== Natural Science == By the 18th century, the rise of [[:en:Natural science|Natural science]] led to a mode of inquiry that excluded subjective meaning and consciousness. Many scholars have noted this as a key characteristic of modern science.
Due to this exclusion, scientific conceptions of nature often appear impoverished. For example, ethical dimensions are absent from scientific understandings of nature.<ref name='josetsu' />
Some proponents of natural science dismiss natural philosophy as outdated.<ref name='josetsu' /> However, the unchecked application of scientific thinking has contributed to global issues such as [[:en:Environmental issues|Environmental problems]].<ref name='josetsu' /> Natural science lacks the means for deep ethical reflection and moral guidance.<ref name='josetsu' />
Since issues like environmental destruction are fundamentally ethical, they require philosophical rather than purely scientific approaches.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Personal Views === Some scientists and researchers in [[:en:Biology|Biology]], particularly [[:en:Ecology|Ecology]], use the term "shizenkan" to describe their personal perspectives on nature. For example, [[:en:Kinji Imanishi|Kinji Imanishi]] published ''Watashi no Shizenkan'' (My View of Nature) in 1978.
== Modern Natural Philosophy ==
