<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:自然観]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:自然観]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:自然観]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11612878]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:22, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shizenkan''' (自然観) refers to the perception of [[:en:Nature|Nature]] as a fundamental value system. It is believed that there are significant differences depending on [[:en:Culture|Bunka]].<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Droz|first=Layna|last2=Chen|first2=Hsun-Mei|last3=Chu|first3=Hung-Tao|last4=Fajrini|first4=Rika|last5=Imbong|first5=Jerry|last6=Jannel|first6=Romaric|last7=Komatsubara|first7=Orika|last8=Lagasca-Hiloma|first8=Concordia Marie A.|last9=Meas|first9=Chansatya|date=2022-05-31|title=Exploring the diversity of conceptualizations of nature in East and South-East Asia|url=https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-022-01186-5|journal=Humanities and Social Sciences Communications|volume=9|issue=1|pages=1–12|language=en|doi=10.1057/s41599-022-01186-5|issn=2662-9992}}</ref>
== Mythological Views of Nature == Among the earliest conceptions of nature are those described in the mythologies of various societies, which illustrate the relationship between humans and nature.
A unique aspect of mythological perceptions of nature is that they also explain history. This is a strength that surpasses even [[:en:Natural philosophy|Philosophical Views of Nature]], which tend to avoid historical explanations.<ref name='josetsu'>{{Cite book|title=Shizen Tetsugaku Josetsu|author=Imamichi Tomonobu|publisher=Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|year=1993}}</ref>
In the {{ill|Judeo-Christian tradition|ja|ユダヤ教・キリスト教圏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Judeo-Christian tradition|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, references to nature can be found in the [[:en:Old Testament|Old Testament]] and [[:en:New Testament|New Testament]], while in the Islamic world, such references appear in the {{ill|Quran|ja|コーラン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Quran|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クルアーン|1=Qur’an|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q428}}. In Japan, natural perceptions from the [[:en:Man'yōshū|Manyoshu]], compiled in ancient times, provide insight into the perspectives of that era.
In {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシャ神話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greek mythology|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ギリシア神話|1=Greek mythology|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q34726}}, nature is depicted through various deities. The wind, for example, had different gods: the north wind was ruled by [[:en:Boreas (disambiguation)|Boreas]], and the west wind by {{ill|Zephyrus|ja|ゼフュリス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zephyrus|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Similarly, fire was governed by its own deity, and even hearth fires had separate divine representations.
== Natural Philosophy == Natural philosophy seeks to discover logical structures and laws governing nature.<ref name='josetsu' /> This field of philosophy extends broadly and, in some contexts, encompasses the entirety of philosophical thought.
{{main|Natural philosophy}}
=== Eastern Views === In [[:en:Chinese philosophy|Chinese philosophy]], it was believed that "pure and light substances rise to form the heavens, while impure and heavy substances descend to form the earth." When applied to the classical [[:en:Big Four|Four Elements]], wind and fire were considered light and rising, while the earth element was regarded as heavy.<ref name='josetsu' />
The [[:en:Liezi|Liezi]], attributed to [[:en:Liezi|Liezi]], posited that everything initially existed in a chaotic state of [[:en:Qi|Qi]].<ref name='josetsu' />
China also developed the concept of [[:en:Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|Five Elements (Wuxing)]], which consists of wood, fire, earth, metal, and water.<ref name='josetsu' /> This theory, rather than defining elements in a material sense, is often interpreted as representing dynamic phenomena.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Western Views === According to [[:en:Aristotle|Aristotle]], the first philosopher to engage in naturalistic inquiry was {{ill|Thales|ja|タレース|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thales|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タレス|1=Thales|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q36303}}, who is considered the originator of "Why?" philosophy. Thales proposed that the fundamental principle, or {{ill|Arche|ja|アルケー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arche|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=arche|WD=Q196638}}, was water.<ref name='josetsu' />
[[:en:Anaximander|Anaximander]] proposed that the Arche was "the infinite (Apeiron)," while {{ill|Anaximenes|ja|アナクシメネース|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anaximenes|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} believed it to be "air." [[:en:Heraclitus|Heraclitus]] asserted that fire was the fundamental principle.<ref name='josetsu' /> These perspectives are sometimes regarded as forms of [[:en:Monism|Monism]].<ref name='josetsu' />
The French philosopher [[:en:Blaise Pascal|Blaise Pascal]] positioned humans between angels and beasts, viewing them as intermediaries between the supernatural and the natural world.<ref>''Pensées'' 358</ref><ref name='josetsu' />
== Natural Science == By the 18th century, the rise of [[:en:Natural science|Natural science]] led to a mode of inquiry that excluded subjective meaning and consciousness. Many scholars have noted this as a key characteristic of modern science.
Due to this exclusion, scientific conceptions of nature often appear impoverished. For example, ethical dimensions are absent from scientific understandings of nature.<ref name='josetsu' />
Some proponents of natural science dismiss natural philosophy as outdated.<ref name='josetsu' /> However, the unchecked application of scientific thinking has contributed to global issues such as [[:en:Environmental issues|Environmental problems]].<ref name='josetsu' /> Natural science lacks the means for deep ethical reflection and moral guidance.<ref name='josetsu' />
Since issues like environmental destruction are fundamentally ethical, they require philosophical rather than purely scientific approaches.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Personal Views === Some scientists and researchers in [[:en:Biology|Biology]], particularly [[:en:Ecology|Ecology]], use the term "shizenkan" to describe their personal perspectives on nature. For example, [[:en:Kinji Imanishi|Kinji Imanishi]] published ''Watashi no Shizenkan'' (My View of Nature) in 1978.

China also developed the concept of [[:en:Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|Five Elements (Wuxing)]], which consists of wood, fire, earth, metal, and water.<ref name='josetsu' /> This theory, rather than defining elements in a material sense, is often interpreted as representing dynamic phenomena.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Western Views === According to [[:en:Aristotle|Aristotle]], the first philosopher to engage in naturalistic inquiry was {{ill|Thales|ja|タレース|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thales|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タレス|1=Thales|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q36303}}, who is considered the originator of "Why?" philosophy. Thales proposed that the fundamental principle, or {{ill|Arche|ja|アルケー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arche|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=arche|WD=Q196638}}, was water.<ref name='josetsu' />
[[:en:Anaximander|Anaximander]] proposed that the Arche was "the infinite (Apeiron)," while {{ill|Anaximenes|ja|アナクシメネース|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anaximenes|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} believed it to be "air." [[:en:Heraclitus|Heraclitus]] asserted that fire was the fundamental principle.<ref name='josetsu' /> These perspectives are sometimes regarded as forms of [[:en:Monism|Monism]].<ref name='josetsu' />
The French philosopher [[:en:Blaise Pascal|Blaise Pascal]] positioned humans between angels and beasts, viewing them as intermediaries between the supernatural and the natural world.<ref>''Pensées'' 358</ref><ref name='josetsu' />
== Natural Science == By the 18th century, the rise of [[:en:Natural science|Natural science]] led to a mode of inquiry that excluded subjective meaning and consciousness. Many scholars have noted this as a key characteristic of modern science.
Due to this exclusion, scientific conceptions of nature often appear impoverished. For example, ethical dimensions are absent from scientific understandings of nature.<ref name='josetsu' />
Some proponents of natural science dismiss natural philosophy as outdated.<ref name='josetsu' /> However, the unchecked application of scientific thinking has contributed to global issues such as [[:en:Environmental issues|Environmental problems]].<ref name='josetsu' /> Natural science lacks the means for deep ethical reflection and moral guidance.<ref name='josetsu' />
Since issues like environmental destruction are fundamentally ethical, they require philosophical rather than purely scientific approaches.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Personal Views === Some scientists and researchers in [[:en:Biology|Biology]], particularly [[:en:Ecology|Ecology]], use the term "shizenkan" to describe their personal perspectives on nature. For example, [[:en:Kinji Imanishi|Kinji Imanishi]] published ''Watashi no Shizenkan'' (My View of Nature) in 1978.
== Modern Natural Philosophy ==
== References == <references/>
{{translated page|ja|自然観|version=95707057|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:自然観]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:自然観]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:自然観]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:自然観]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11612878]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:22, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shizenkan''' (自然観) refers to the perception of {{ill|Nature|en|Nature|WD=Q7860|lt=Nature}} as a fundamental value system. It is believed that there are significant differences depending on {{ill|Culture|en|Culture|WD=Q11042|lt=Bunka}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Droz|first=Layna|last2=Chen|first2=Hsun-Mei|last3=Chu|first3=Hung-Tao|last4=Fajrini|first4=Rika|last5=Imbong|first5=Jerry|last6=Jannel|first6=Romaric|last7=Komatsubara|first7=Orika|last8=Lagasca-Hiloma|first8=Concordia Marie A.|last9=Meas|first9=Chansatya|date=2022-05-31|title=Exploring the diversity of conceptualizations of nature in East and South-East Asia|url=https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-022-01186-5|journal=Humanities and Social Sciences Communications|volume=9|issue=1|pages=1–12|language=en|doi=10.1057/s41599-022-01186-5|issn=2662-9992}}</ref>
== Mythological Views of Nature == Among the earliest conceptions of nature are those described in the mythologies of various societies, which illustrate the relationship between humans and nature.
A unique aspect of mythological perceptions of nature is that they also explain history. This is a strength that surpasses even {{ill|Natural philosophy|en|Natural philosophy|WD=Q269323|lt=Philosophical Views of Nature}}, which tend to avoid historical explanations.<ref name='josetsu'>{{Cite book|title=Shizen Tetsugaku Josetsu|author=Imamichi Tomonobu|publisher=Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|year=1993}}</ref>
In the {{ill|Judeo-Christian tradition|ja|ユダヤ教・キリスト教圏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Judeo-Christian tradition|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, references to nature can be found in the {{ill|Old Testament|en|Old Testament|WD=Q19786|lt=Old Testament}} and {{ill|New Testament|en|New Testament|WD=Q18813|lt=New Testament}}, while in the Islamic world, such references appear in the {{ill|Quran|ja|コーラン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Quran|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クルアーン|1=Qur’an|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q428}}. In Japan, natural perceptions from the {{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|WD=Q834321|lt=Manyoshu}}, compiled in ancient times, provide insight into the perspectives of that era.
In {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシャ神話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greek mythology|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ギリシア神話|1=Greek mythology|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q34726}}, nature is depicted through various deities. The wind, for example, had different gods: the north wind was ruled by {{ill|Boreas (disambiguation)|en|Boreas (disambiguation)|WD=Q356630|lt=Boreas}}, and the west wind by {{ill|Zephyrus|ja|ゼフュリス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zephyrus|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Similarly, fire was governed by its own deity, and even hearth fires had separate divine representations.
== Natural Philosophy == Natural philosophy seeks to discover logical structures and laws governing nature.<ref name='josetsu' /> This field of philosophy extends broadly and, in some contexts, encompasses the entirety of philosophical thought.
{{main|Natural philosophy}}
=== Eastern Views === In {{ill|Chinese philosophy|en|Chinese philosophy|WD=Q184663|lt=Chinese philosophy}}, it was believed that "pure and light substances rise to form the heavens, while impure and heavy substances descend to form the earth." When applied to the classical {{ill|Big Four|en|Big Four|WD=Q858894|lt=Four Elements}}, wind and fire were considered light and rising, while the earth element was regarded as heavy.<ref name='josetsu' />
The {{ill|Liezi|en|Liezi|WD=Q434429|lt=Liezi}}, attributed to {{ill|Liezi|en|Liezi|WD=Q434429|lt=Liezi}}, posited that everything initially existed in a chaotic state of {{ill|Qi|en|Qi|WD=Q202742|lt=Qi}}.<ref name='josetsu' />
China also developed the concept of {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|en|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|WD=Q218652|lt=Five Elements (Wuxing)}}, which consists of wood, fire, earth, metal, and water.<ref name='josetsu' /> This theory, rather than defining elements in a material sense, is often interpreted as representing dynamic phenomena.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Western Views === According to {{ill|Aristotle|en|Aristotle|WD=Q868|lt=Aristotle}}, the first philosopher to engage in naturalistic inquiry was {{ill|Thales|ja|タレース|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thales|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タレス|1=Thales|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q36303}}, who is considered the originator of "Why?" philosophy. Thales proposed that the fundamental principle, or {{ill|Arche|ja|アルケー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arche|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=arche|WD=Q196638}}, was water.<ref name='josetsu' />
{{ill|Anaximander|en|Anaximander|WD=Q42458|lt=Anaximander}} proposed that the Arche was "the infinite (Apeiron)," while {{ill|Anaximenes|ja|アナクシメネース|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anaximenes|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} believed it to be "air." {{ill|Heraclitus|en|Heraclitus|WD=Q41155|lt=Heraclitus}} asserted that fire was the fundamental principle.<ref name='josetsu' /> These perspectives are sometimes regarded as forms of {{ill|Monism|en|Monism|WD=Q178801|lt=Monism}}.<ref name='josetsu' />
The French philosopher {{ill|Blaise Pascal|en|Blaise Pascal|WD=Q1290|lt=Blaise Pascal}} positioned humans between angels and beasts, viewing them as intermediaries between the supernatural and the natural world.<ref>''Pensées'' 358</ref><ref name='josetsu' />
== Natural Science == By the 18th century, the rise of {{ill|Natural science|en|Natural science|WD=Q7991|lt=Natural science}} led to a mode of inquiry that excluded subjective meaning and consciousness. Many scholars have noted this as a key characteristic of modern science.
Due to this exclusion, scientific conceptions of nature often appear impoverished. For example, ethical dimensions are absent from scientific understandings of nature.<ref name='josetsu' />
Some proponents of natural science dismiss natural philosophy as outdated.<ref name='josetsu' /> However, the unchecked application of scientific thinking has contributed to global issues such as {{ill|Environmental issues|en|Environmental issues|WD=Q2144359|lt=Environmental problems}}.<ref name='josetsu' /> Natural science lacks the means for deep ethical reflection and moral guidance.<ref name='josetsu' />
Since issues like environmental destruction are fundamentally ethical, they require philosophical rather than purely scientific approaches.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Personal Views === Some scientists and researchers in {{ill|Biology|en|Biology|WD=Q420|lt=Biology}}, particularly {{ill|Ecology|en|Ecology|WD=Q7150|lt=Ecology}}, use the term "shizenkan" to describe their personal perspectives on nature. For example, {{ill|Kinji Imanishi|en|Kinji Imanishi|WD=Q788092|lt=Kinji Imanishi}} published ''Watashi no Shizenkan'' (My View of Nature) in 1978.
== Modern Natural Philosophy ==
== References == <references/>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:自然観]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:自然観]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:自然観]]
'''Shizenkan''' (自然観) refers to the perception of {{ill|Nature|en|Nature|WD=Q7860|lt=Nature}} as a fundamental value system. It is believed that there are significant differences depending on {{ill|Culture|en|Culture|WD=Q11042|lt=Bunka}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Droz|first=Layna|last2=Chen|first2=Hsun-Mei|last3=Chu|first3=Hung-Tao|last4=Fajrini|first4=Rika|last5=Imbong|first5=Jerry|last6=Jannel|first6=Romaric|last7=Komatsubara|first7=Orika|last8=Lagasca-Hiloma|first8=Concordia Marie A.|last9=Meas|first9=Chansatya|date=2022-05-31|title=Exploring the diversity of conceptualizations of nature in East and South-East Asia|url=https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-022-01186-5|journal=Humanities and Social Sciences Communications|volume=9|issue=1|pages=1–12|language=en|doi=10.1057/s41599-022-01186-5|issn=2662-9992}}</ref>
== Mythological Views of Nature == Among the earliest conceptions of nature are those described in the mythologies of various societies, which illustrate the relationship between humans and nature.
A unique aspect of mythological perceptions of nature is that they also explain history. This is a strength that surpasses even {{ill|Natural philosophy|en|Natural philosophy|WD=Q269323|lt=Philosophical Views of Nature}}, which tend to avoid historical explanations.<ref name='josetsu'>{{Cite book|title=Shizen Tetsugaku Josetsu|author=Imamichi Tomonobu|publisher=Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|year=1993}}</ref>
In the {{ill|Judeo-Christian tradition|ja|ユダヤ教・キリスト教圏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Judeo-Christian tradition|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, references to nature can be found in the {{ill|Old Testament|en|Old Testament|WD=Q19786|lt=Old Testament}} and {{ill|New Testament|en|New Testament|WD=Q18813|lt=New Testament}}, while in the Islamic world, such references appear in the {{ill|Quran|ja|コーラン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Quran|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クルアーン|1=Qur’an|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q428}}. In Japan, natural perceptions from the {{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|WD=Q834321|lt=Manyoshu}}, compiled in ancient times, provide insight into the perspectives of that era.
In {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシャ神話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greek mythology|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ギリシア神話|1=Greek mythology|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q34726}}, nature is depicted through various deities. The wind, for example, had different gods: the north wind was ruled by {{ill|Boreas (disambiguation)|en|Boreas (disambiguation)|WD=Q356630|lt=Boreas}}, and the west wind by {{ill|Zephyrus|ja|ゼフュリス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zephyrus|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Similarly, fire was governed by its own deity, and even hearth fires had separate divine representations.
== Natural Philosophy == Natural philosophy seeks to discover logical structures and laws governing nature.<ref name='josetsu' /> This field of philosophy extends broadly and, in some contexts, encompasses the entirety of philosophical thought.
{{main|Natural philosophy}}
=== Eastern Views === In {{ill|Chinese philosophy|en|Chinese philosophy|WD=Q184663|lt=Chinese philosophy}}, it was believed that "pure and light substances rise to form the heavens, while impure and heavy substances descend to form the earth." When applied to the classical {{ill|Big Four|en|Big Four|WD=Q858894|lt=Four Elements}}, wind and fire were considered light and rising, while the earth element was regarded as heavy.<ref name='josetsu' />
The {{ill|Liezi|en|Liezi|WD=Q434429|lt=Liezi}}, attributed to {{ill|Liezi|en|Liezi|WD=Q434429|lt=Liezi}}, posited that everything initially existed in a chaotic state of {{ill|Qi|en|Qi|WD=Q202742|lt=Qi}}.<ref name='josetsu' />
China also developed the concept of {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|en|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|WD=Q218652|lt=Five Elements (Wuxing)}}, which consists of wood, fire, earth, metal, and water.<ref name='josetsu' /> This theory, rather than defining elements in a material sense, is often interpreted as representing dynamic phenomena.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Western Views === According to {{ill|Aristotle|en|Aristotle|WD=Q868|lt=Aristotle}}, the first philosopher to engage in naturalistic inquiry was {{ill|Thales|ja|タレース|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thales|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タレス|1=Thales|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q36303}}, who is considered the originator of "Why?" philosophy. Thales proposed that the fundamental principle, or {{ill|Arche|ja|アルケー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arche|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=arche|WD=Q196638}}, was water.<ref name='josetsu' />
{{ill|Anaximander|en|Anaximander|WD=Q42458|lt=Anaximander}} proposed that the Arche was "the infinite (Apeiron)," while {{ill|Anaximenes|ja|アナクシメネース|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anaximenes|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} believed it to be "air." {{ill|Heraclitus|en|Heraclitus|WD=Q41155|lt=Heraclitus}} asserted that fire was the fundamental principle.<ref name='josetsu' /> These perspectives are sometimes regarded as forms of {{ill|Monism|en|Monism|WD=Q178801|lt=Monism}}.<ref name='josetsu' />
The French philosopher {{ill|Blaise Pascal|en|Blaise Pascal|WD=Q1290|lt=Blaise Pascal}} positioned humans between angels and beasts, viewing them as intermediaries between the supernatural and the natural world.<ref>''Pensées'' 358</ref><ref name='josetsu' />
== Natural Science == By the 18th century, the rise of {{ill|Natural science|en|Natural science|WD=Q7991|lt=Natural science}} led to a mode of inquiry that excluded subjective meaning and consciousness. Many scholars have noted this as a key characteristic of modern science.
Due to this exclusion, scientific conceptions of nature often appear impoverished. For example, ethical dimensions are absent from scientific understandings of nature.<ref name='josetsu' />
Some proponents of natural science dismiss natural philosophy as outdated.<ref name='josetsu' /> However, the unchecked application of scientific thinking has contributed to global issues such as {{ill|Environmental issues|en|Environmental issues|WD=Q2144359|lt=Environmental problems}}.<ref name='josetsu' /> Natural science lacks the means for deep ethical reflection and moral guidance.<ref name='josetsu' />
Since issues like environmental destruction are fundamentally ethical, they require philosophical rather than purely scientific approaches.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Personal Views === Some scientists and researchers in {{ill|Biology|en|Biology|WD=Q420|lt=Biology}}, particularly {{ill|Ecology|en|Ecology|WD=Q7150|lt=Ecology}}, use the term "shizenkan" to describe their personal perspectives on nature. For example, {{ill|Kinji Imanishi|en|Kinji Imanishi|WD=Q788092|lt=Kinji Imanishi}} published ''Watashi no Shizenkan'' (My View of Nature) in 1978.
== Modern Natural Philosophy ==
== References == <references/>
{{translated page|ja|自然観|version=95707057|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:自然観]]

China also developed the concept of {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|en|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|WD=Q218652|lt=Five Elements (Wuxing)}}, which consists of wood, fire, earth, metal, and water.<ref name='josetsu' /> This theory, rather than defining elements in a material sense, is often interpreted as representing dynamic phenomena.<ref name='josetsu' />

Thales proposed that the fundamental principle, or {{ill|Arche|ja|アルケー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arche|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=arche|WD=Q196638}}, was water.<ref name='josetsu' />
{{ill|Anaximander|en|Anaximander|WD=Q42458|lt=Anaximander}} proposed that the Arche was "the infinite (Apeiron)," while {{ill|Anaximenes|ja|アナクシメネース|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anaximenes|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} believed it to be "air." {{ill|Heraclitus|en|Heraclitus|WD=Q41155|lt=Heraclitus}} asserted that fire was the fundamental principle.<ref name='josetsu' /> These perspectives are sometimes regarded as forms of {{ill|Monism|en|Monism|WD=Q178801|lt=Monism}}.<ref name='josetsu' />
The French philosopher {{ill|Blaise Pascal|en|Blaise Pascal|WD=Q1290|lt=Blaise Pascal}} positioned humans between angels and beasts, viewing them as intermediaries between the supernatural and the natural world.<ref>''Pensées'' 358</ref><ref name='josetsu' />
== Natural Science == By the 18th century, the rise of {{ill|Natural science|en|Natural science|WD=Q7991|lt=Natural science}} led to a mode of inquiry that excluded subjective meaning and consciousness. Many scholars have noted this as a key characteristic of modern science.
Due to this exclusion, scientific conceptions of nature often appear impoverished. For example, ethical dimensions are absent from scientific understandings of nature.<ref name='josetsu' />
Some proponents of natural science dismiss natural philosophy as outdated.<ref name='josetsu' /> However, the unchecked application of scientific thinking has contributed to global issues such as {{ill|Environmental issues|en|Environmental issues|WD=Q2144359|lt=Environmental problems}}.<ref name='josetsu' /> Natural science lacks the means for deep ethical reflection and moral guidance.<ref name='josetsu' />
Since issues like environmental destruction are fundamentally ethical, they require philosophical rather than purely scientific approaches.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Personal Views === Some scientists and researchers in {{ill|Biology|en|Biology|WD=Q420|lt=Biology}}, particularly {{ill|Ecology|en|Ecology|WD=Q7150|lt=Ecology}}, use the term "shizenkan" to describe their personal perspectives on nature. For example, {{ill|Kinji Imanishi|en|Kinji Imanishi|WD=Q788092|lt=Kinji Imanishi}} published ''Watashi no Shizenkan'' (My View of Nature) in 1978.
== Modern Natural Philosophy ==
== References == <references/>
{{translated page|ja|自然観|version=95707057|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:自然観]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:自然観]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:自然観]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:自然観]]
'''Shizenkan''' (自然観) refers to the perception of {{ill|Nature|en|Nature|WD=Q7860|lt=Nature|1=Nature}} as a fundamental value system. It is believed that there are significant differences depending on {{ill|Culture|en|Culture|WD=Q11042|lt=Bunka|1=Culture}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Droz|first=Layna|last2=Chen|first2=Hsun-Mei|last3=Chu|first3=Hung-Tao|last4=Fajrini|first4=Rika|last5=Imbong|first5=Jerry|last6=Jannel|first6=Romaric|last7=Komatsubara|first7=Orika|last8=Lagasca-Hiloma|first8=Concordia Marie A.|last9=Meas|first9=Chansatya|date=2022-05-31|title=Exploring the diversity of conceptualizations of nature in East and South-East Asia|url=https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-022-01186-5|journal=Humanities and Social Sciences Communications|volume=9|issue=1|pages=1–12|language=en|doi=10.1057/s41599-022-01186-5|issn=2662-9992}}</ref>
== Mythological Views of Nature == Among the earliest conceptions of nature are those described in the mythologies of various societies, which illustrate the relationship between humans and nature.
A unique aspect of mythological perceptions of nature is that they also explain history. This is a strength that surpasses even {{ill|Natural philosophy|en|Natural philosophy|WD=Q269323|lt=Philosophical Views of Nature|1=Natural philosophy}}, which tend to avoid historical explanations.<ref name='josetsu'>{{Cite book|title=Shizen Tetsugaku Josetsu|author=Imamichi Tomonobu|publisher=Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|year=1993}}</ref>
In the {{ill|Judeo-Christian tradition|ja|ユダヤ教・キリスト教圏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Judeo-Christian tradition|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, references to nature can be found in the {{ill|Old Testament|en|Old Testament|WD=Q19786|lt=Old Testament|1=Old Testament}} and {{ill|New Testament|en|New Testament|WD=Q18813|lt=New Testament|1=New Testament}}, while in the Islamic world, such references appear in the {{ill|Quran|ja|コーラン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Quran|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クルアーン|1=Quran|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q428}}. In Japan, natural perceptions from the {{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|WD=Q834321|lt=Manyoshu|1=Man'yōshū}}, compiled in ancient times, provide insight into the perspectives of that era.
In {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシャ神話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greek mythology|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ギリシア神話|1=Greek mythology|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q34726}}, nature is depicted through various deities. The wind, for example, had different gods: the north wind was ruled by {{ill|Boreas (disambiguation)|en|Boreas (disambiguation)|WD=Q356630|lt=Boreas|1=Boreas (disambiguation)}}, and the west wind by {{ill|Zephyrus|ja|ゼフュリス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zephyrus|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Similarly, fire was governed by its own deity, and even hearth fires had separate divine representations.
== Natural Philosophy == Natural philosophy seeks to discover logical structures and laws governing nature.<ref name='josetsu' /> This field of philosophy extends broadly and, in some contexts, encompasses the entirety of philosophical thought.
{{main|Natural philosophy}}
=== Eastern Views === In {{ill|Chinese philosophy|en|Chinese philosophy|WD=Q184663|lt=Chinese philosophy|1=Chinese philosophy}}, it was believed that "pure and light substances rise to form the heavens, while impure and heavy substances descend to form the earth." When applied to the classical {{ill|Big Four|en|Big Four|WD=Q858894|lt=Four Elements|1=Big Four}}, wind and fire were considered light and rising, while the earth element was regarded as heavy.<ref name='josetsu' />
The {{ill|Liezi|en|Liezi|WD=Q434429|lt=Liezi|1=Liezi}}, attributed to {{ill|Liezi|en|Liezi|WD=Q434429|lt=Liezi|1=Liezi}}, posited that everything initially existed in a chaotic state of {{ill|Qi|en|Qi|WD=Q202742|lt=Qi|1=Qi}}.<ref name='josetsu' />
China also developed the concept of {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|en|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|WD=Q218652|lt=Five Elements (Wuxing)|1=Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)}}, which consists of wood, fire, earth, metal, and water.<ref name='josetsu' /> This theory, rather than defining elements in a material sense, is often interpreted as representing dynamic phenomena.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Western Views === According to {{ill|Aristotle|en|Aristotle|WD=Q868|lt=Aristotle|1=Aristotle}}, the first philosopher to engage in naturalistic inquiry was {{ill|Thales|ja|タレース|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thales|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タレス|1=Thales of Miletus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q36303}}, who is considered the originator of "Why?" philosophy. Thales proposed that the fundamental principle, or {{ill|Arche|ja|アルケー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arche|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Arche|WD=Q196638}}, was water.<ref name='josetsu' />
{{ill|Anaximander|en|Anaximander|WD=Q42458|lt=Anaximander|1=Anaximander}} proposed that the Arche was "the infinite (Apeiron)," while {{ill|Anaximenes|ja|アナクシメネース|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anaximenes|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} believed it to be "air." {{ill|Heraclitus|en|Heraclitus|WD=Q41155|lt=Heraclitus|1=Heraclitus}} asserted that fire was the fundamental principle.<ref name='josetsu' /> These perspectives are sometimes regarded as forms of {{ill|Monism|en|Monism|WD=Q178801|lt=Monism|1=Monism}}.<ref name='josetsu' />
The French philosopher {{ill|Blaise Pascal|en|Blaise Pascal|WD=Q1290|lt=Blaise Pascal|1=Blaise Pascal}} positioned humans between angels and beasts, viewing them as intermediaries between the supernatural and the natural world.<ref>''Pensées'' 358</ref><ref name='josetsu' />
== Natural Science == By the 18th century, the rise of {{ill|Natural science|en|Natural science|WD=Q7991|lt=Natural science|1=Natural science}} led to a mode of inquiry that excluded subjective meaning and consciousness. Many scholars have noted this as a key characteristic of modern science.
Due to this exclusion, scientific conceptions of nature often appear impoverished. For example, ethical dimensions are absent from scientific understandings of nature.<ref name='josetsu' />
Some proponents of natural science dismiss natural philosophy as outdated.<ref name='josetsu' /> However, the unchecked application of scientific thinking has contributed to global issues such as {{ill|Environmental issues|en|Environmental issues|WD=Q2144359|lt=Environmental problems|1=Environmental issues}}.<ref name='josetsu' /> Natural science lacks the means for deep ethical reflection and moral guidance.<ref name='josetsu' />
Since issues like environmental destruction are fundamentally ethical, they require philosophical rather than purely scientific approaches.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Personal Views === Some scientists and researchers in {{ill|Biology|en|Biology|WD=Q420|lt=Biology|1=Biology}}, particularly {{ill|Ecology|en|Ecology|WD=Q7150|lt=Ecology|1=Ecology}}, use the term "shizenkan" to describe their personal perspectives on nature. For example, {{ill|Kinji Imanishi|en|Kinji Imanishi|WD=Q788092|lt=Kinji Imanishi|1=Kinji Imanishi}} published ''Watashi no Shizenkan'' (My View of Nature) in 1978.
== Modern Natural Philosophy ==

revision.text:
'''Shizenkan''' (自然観) refers to the perception of {{ill|Nature|en|Nature|WD=Q7860|lt=Nature|1=Nature}} as a fundamental value system. It is believed that there are significant differences depending on {{ill|Culture|en|Culture|WD=Q11042|lt=Bunka|1=Culture}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Droz|first=Layna|last2=Chen|first2=Hsun-Mei|last3=Chu|first3=Hung-Tao|last4=Fajrini|first4=Rika|last5=Imbong|first5=Jerry|last6=Jannel|first6=Romaric|last7=Komatsubara|first7=Orika|last8=Lagasca-Hiloma|first8=Concordia Marie A.|last9=Meas|first9=Chansatya|date=2022-05-31|title=Exploring the diversity of conceptualizations of nature in East and South-East Asia|url=https://www.nature.com/articles/s41599-022-01186-5|journal=Humanities and Social Sciences Communications|volume=9|issue=1|pages=1–12|language=en|doi=10.1057/s41599-022-01186-5|issn=2662-9992}}</ref>
== Mythological Views of Nature == Among the earliest conceptions of nature are those described in the mythologies of various societies, which illustrate the relationship between humans and nature.
A unique aspect of mythological perceptions of nature is that they also explain history. This is a strength that surpasses even {{ill|Natural philosophy|en|Natural philosophy|WD=Q269323|lt=Philosophical Views of Nature|1=Natural philosophy}}, which tend to avoid historical explanations.<ref name='josetsu'>{{Cite book|title=Shizen Tetsugaku Josetsu|author=Imamichi Tomonobu|publisher=Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|year=1993}}</ref>
In the {{ill|Judeo-Christian tradition|ja|ユダヤ教・キリスト教圏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Judeo-Christian tradition|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, references to nature can be found in the {{ill|Old Testament|en|Old Testament|WD=Q19786|lt=Old Testament|1=Old Testament}} and {{ill|New Testament|en|New Testament|WD=Q18813|lt=New Testament|1=New Testament}}, while in the Islamic world, such references appear in the {{ill|Quran|ja|コーラン|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Quran|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to クルアーン|1=Quran|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q428}}. In Japan, natural perceptions from the {{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|WD=Q834321|lt=Manyoshu|1=Man'yōshū}}, compiled in ancient times, provide insight into the perspectives of that era.
In {{ill|Greek mythology|ja|ギリシャ神話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Greek mythology|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ギリシア神話|1=Greek mythology|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q34726}}, nature is depicted through various deities. The wind, for example, had different gods: the north wind was ruled by {{ill|Boreas (disambiguation)|en|Boreas (disambiguation)|WD=Q356630|lt=Boreas|1=Boreas (disambiguation)}}, and the west wind by {{ill|Zephyrus|ja|ゼフュリス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zephyrus|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Similarly, fire was governed by its own deity, and even hearth fires had separate divine representations.
== Natural Philosophy == Natural philosophy seeks to discover logical structures and laws governing nature.<ref name='josetsu' /> This field of philosophy extends broadly and, in some contexts, encompasses the entirety of philosophical thought.
{{main|Natural philosophy}}
=== Eastern Views === In {{ill|Chinese philosophy|en|Chinese philosophy|WD=Q184663|lt=Chinese philosophy|1=Chinese philosophy}}, it was believed that "pure and light substances rise to form the heavens, while impure and heavy substances descend to form the earth." When applied to the classical {{ill|Big Four|en|Big Four|WD=Q858894|lt=Four Elements|1=Big Four}}, wind and fire were considered light and rising, while the earth element was regarded as heavy.<ref name='josetsu' />
The {{ill|Liezi|en|Liezi|WD=Q434429|lt=Liezi|1=Liezi}}, attributed to {{ill|Liezi|en|Liezi|WD=Q434429|lt=Liezi|1=Liezi}}, posited that everything initially existed in a chaotic state of {{ill|Qi|en|Qi|WD=Q202742|lt=Qi|1=Qi}}.<ref name='josetsu' />
China also developed the concept of {{ill|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|en|Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)|WD=Q218652|lt=Five Elements (Wuxing)|1=Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)}}, which consists of wood, fire, earth, metal, and water.<ref name='josetsu' /> This theory, rather than defining elements in a material sense, is often interpreted as representing dynamic phenomena.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Western Views === According to {{ill|Aristotle|en|Aristotle|WD=Q868|lt=Aristotle|1=Aristotle}}, the first philosopher to engage in naturalistic inquiry was {{ill|Thales|ja|タレース|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thales|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タレス|1=Thales of Miletus|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q36303}}, who is considered the originator of "Why?" philosophy. Thales proposed that the fundamental principle, or {{ill|Arche|ja|アルケー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arche|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Arche|WD=Q196638}}, was water.<ref name='josetsu' />
{{ill|Anaximander|en|Anaximander|WD=Q42458|lt=Anaximander|1=Anaximander}} proposed that the Arche was "the infinite (Apeiron)," while {{ill|Anaximenes|ja|アナクシメネース|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anaximenes|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} believed it to be "air." {{ill|Heraclitus|en|Heraclitus|WD=Q41155|lt=Heraclitus|1=Heraclitus}} asserted that fire was the fundamental principle.<ref name='josetsu' /> These perspectives are sometimes regarded as forms of {{ill|Monism|en|Monism|WD=Q178801|lt=Monism|1=Monism}}.<ref name='josetsu' />
The French philosopher {{ill|Blaise Pascal|en|Blaise Pascal|WD=Q1290|lt=Blaise Pascal|1=Blaise Pascal}} positioned humans between angels and beasts, viewing them as intermediaries between the supernatural and the natural world.<ref>''Pensées'' 358</ref><ref name='josetsu' />
== Natural Science == By the 18th century, the rise of {{ill|Natural science|en|Natural science|WD=Q7991|lt=Natural science|1=Natural science}} led to a mode of inquiry that excluded subjective meaning and consciousness. Many scholars have noted this as a key characteristic of modern science.
Due to this exclusion, scientific conceptions of nature often appear impoverished. For example, ethical dimensions are absent from scientific understandings of nature.<ref name='josetsu' />
Some proponents of natural science dismiss natural philosophy as outdated.<ref name='josetsu' /> However, the unchecked application of scientific thinking has contributed to global issues such as {{ill|Environmental issues|en|Environmental issues|WD=Q2144359|lt=Environmental problems|1=Environmental issues}}.<ref name='josetsu' /> Natural science lacks the means for deep ethical reflection and moral guidance.<ref name='josetsu' />
Since issues like environmental destruction are fundamentally ethical, they require philosophical rather than purely scientific approaches.<ref name='josetsu' />
=== Personal Views === Some scientists and researchers in {{ill|Biology|en|Biology|WD=Q420|lt=Biology|1=Biology}}, particularly {{ill|Ecology|en|Ecology|WD=Q7150|lt=Ecology|1=Ecology}}, use the term "shizenkan" to describe their personal perspectives on nature. For example, {{ill|Kinji Imanishi|en|Kinji Imanishi|WD=Q788092|lt=Kinji Imanishi|1=Kinji Imanishi}} published ''Watashi no Shizenkan'' (My View of Nature) in 1978.
